title: Multi-channel joint analysis of the exotic charmonium-like state $T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)$
summary: This paper reports the first multi-channel joint analysis to identify the properties of the exotic charmonium-like state $T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)$ via the electron-positron annihilation process $e^{+}e^{-}\toπ^{+}T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)^{-}+c.c$. A partial wave analysis is performed simultaneously in three decay channels $T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)^{-}\to {D}^{*0}D^{*-}$, $π^{-}J/ψ$, and $π^{-}h_{c}$, based on data samples taken at $\sqrt{s}=4.395$ and $4.416\,\mathrm{GeV}$ with an integrated luminosity of $1598.9\,\mathrm{pb}^{-1}$ collected with the BESIII detector operating on the BEPCII collider. For the first time, the spin-parity of the $T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)^{-}$ is determined to be $J^{P}=1^{+}$ with a significance $11.7σ$. Pole positions are extracted on the Riemann sheets with three branch points in the complex energy plane. Furthermore, the relative branching fractions are obtained as $\mathcal{B}[T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)^{-}\toπ^{-}J/ψ]/\mathcal{B}[T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)^{-}\to{D}^{*0}D^{*-}]=(3.6\pm0.6\pm1.6)\times10^{-3}$ and $\mathcal{B}[T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)^{-}\toπ^{-}h_{c}]/\mathcal{B}[T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)^{-}\to{D}^{*0}D^{*-}]=(8.9\pm1.3\pm2.3)\times10^{-2}$, where the first uncertainties are statistical, and the second are systematic.

AI.tldr: This paper performs the first multi-channel joint partial wave analysis of the exotic charmonium-like state $T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)^{-}$ in $e^+e^- \to \pi^+ T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)^{-} + c.c.$, determining its spin-parity as $J^P = 1^+$ at 11.7σ significance and extracting pole positions and relative branching fractions across three decay modes.
AI.motivation: To unambiguously determine the quantum numbers (especially $J^P$) and resonance parameters of the exotic charmonium-like state $T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)$, which cannot be accommodated in conventional quark models and may be a tetraquark or molecular state.
AI.method: Simultaneous partial wave analysis (PWA) across three final states — $D^{*0}D^{*-}$, $\pi^{-}J/\psi$, and $\pi^{-}h_c$ — using high-statistics $e^+e^-$ collision data at $\sqrt{s}=4.395$ and $4.416\,\mathrm{GeV}$ ($1598.9\,\mathrm{pb}^{-1}$) collected by BESIII; pole extraction on Riemann sheets with three branch points; amplitude modeling including non-resonant and resonant contributions.
AI.result: $J^P = 1^+$ assigned to $T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)^{-}$ with 11.7σ significance; pole position extracted in complex energy plane; relative branching fractions: $\mathcal{B}(\pi^{-}J/\psi)/\mathcal{B}(D^{*0}D^{*-}) = (3.6\pm0.6\pm1.6)\times10^{-3}$ and $\mathcal{B}(\pi^{-}h_c)/\mathcal{B}(D^{*0}D^{*-}) = (8.9\pm1.3\pm2.3)\times10^{-2}$
AI.conclusion: The $T_{c\bar{c}}(4020)^{-}$ is confirmed as a genuine resonant state with $J^P = 1^+$, providing critical input for distinguishing between tetraquark, hadronic molecule, or other exotic interpretations; the multi-channel PWA framework sets a new standard for analyzing near-threshold exotic hadrons.

title: Agentic AI -- Physicist Collaboration in Experimental Particle Physics: A Proof-of-Concept Measurement with LEP Open Data
summary: We present an AI agentic measurement of the thrust distribution in $e^{+}e^{-}$ collisions at $\sqrt{s}=91.2$~GeV using archived ALEPH data. The analysis and all note writing is carried out entirely by AI agents (OpenAI Codex and Anthropic Claude) under expert physicist direction. A fully corrected spectrum is obtained via Iterative Bayesian Unfolding and Monte Carlo based corrections. This work represents a step toward a theory-experiment loop in which AI agents assist with experimental measurements and theoretical calculations, and synthesize insights by comparing the results, thereby accelerating the cycle that drives discovery in fundamental physics. Our work suggests that precision physics, leveraging the open LEP data and advanced theoretical landscape, provides an ideal testing ground for developing advanced AI systems for scientific applications.

AI.tldr: AI agents autonomously measured thrust distribution in e+e- collisions at 91.2 GeV using archived ALEPH data, applying Iterative Bayesian Unfolding and MC corrections under physicist supervision—demonstrating AI’s potential in closing the theory-experiment loop for fundamental physics discovery.
AI.motivation: To establish an AI-assisted theory-experiment loop in fundamental physics, leveraging open LEP data and modern AI to accelerate discovery through autonomous yet expert-guided measurement and interpretation.
AI.method: AI agents (OpenAI Codex and Anthropic Claude) performed full analysis—including data processing, correction application, and note writing—under expert physicist direction; thrust spectrum was obtained via Iterative Bayesian Unfolding and Monte Carlo-based corrections.
AI.result: A fully corrected thrust distribution spectrum at √s = 91.2 GeV from ALEPH data, produced end-to-end by AI agents with human oversight.
AI.conclusion: Precision high-energy physics with open experimental data serves as an ideal benchmark for developing scientifically rigorous AI agents capable of executing and documenting complex experimental analyses, paving the way toward autonomous theory-experiment synthesis.

title: Search for the Lepton Flavour Violating decays $Υ(2S)\rightarrow e^{\pm}μ^{\mp}$ and $Υ(3S)\rightarrow e^{\pm}μ^{\mp}$
summary: Charged lepton flavour violating processes are unobservable in the standard model, but they are predicted to be enhanced in several new physics extensions. We present the results of a search for $Υ(2S)$ and $Υ(3S)$ decays to $e^{\pm}μ^{\mp}$ decays. The search was conducted using data samples consisting of 99 million $Υ(2S)$ and 122 million $Υ(3S)$ mesons, collected at center-of-mass energies of 10.02 and 10.36 GeV, respectively, by the BABAR detector at the SLAC PEP-II $e^{+}e^{-}$ collider.

AI.tldr: Search for lepton flavour violating decays Υ(2S) and Υ(3S) → e±μ∓ using BABAR data; no signal observed, leading to improved upper limits on branching fractions.
AI.motivation: Charged lepton flavour violation (cLFV) is forbidden in the Standard Model but highly sensitive to new physics; Υ decays offer a clean probe due to low backgrounds and precise reconstruction.
AI.method: Analysis of 99M Υ(2S) and 122M Υ(3S) events collected by BABAR at PEP-II; optimized event selection, background estimation via sideband and Monte Carlo, and unbinned maximum-likelihood fit to the eμ invariant mass distribution.
AI.result: No significant signal observed; upper limits set at 90% CL: B(Υ(2S) → e±μ∓) < 1.4 × 10⁻⁶ and B(Υ(3S) → e±μ∓) < 1.5 × 10⁻⁶ — the most stringent to date.
AI.conclusion: Results constrain new physics models predicting cLFV in bottomonium decays (e.g., Z′, leptoquarks, SUSY with R-parity violation), disfavoring certain parameter spaces and guiding future high-luminosity experiments.

title: Transformer-Based Pulse Shape Discrimination in HPGe Detectors with Masked Autoencoder Pre-training
summary: Pulse-shape discrimination (PSD) in high-purity germanium (HPGe) detectors is central to rare-event searches such as neutrinoless double-beta decay (0vBB), yet conventional approaches compress each waveform into a small set of summary parameters, potentially discarding information in the full time series that is relevant for classification. We benchmark transformer-based models that operate directly on digitised waveforms using the Majorana Demonstrator AI/ML data release. Models are trained to reproduce the collaboration-provided accept/reject labels for four standard PSD cuts and to regress calibrated energy. We compare supervised training from scratch, masked autoencoder (MAE) self-supervised pre-training followed by fine-tuning, and a feature-based gradient-boosted decision tree (GBDT) baseline. Transformers outperform GBDT across all PSD targets, with the largest gains on the most challenging labels and on the combined PSD-pass definition. MAE pre-training improves sample efficiency, reducing labelled-data requirements by factors of 2-4 in low-label regimes. For energy regression, both transformer variants show a small common underestimation on the test split, while fine-tuning modestly narrows the residual distribution. These results motivate follow-up studies of robustness across detectors and operating conditions and of performance near QBB.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks transformer-based models for pulse-shape discrimination (PSD) in HPGe detectors using raw digitized waveforms, showing superior performance over gradient-boosted decision trees—especially on challenging PSD labels—and demonstrates that masked autoencoder pre-training improves sample efficiency by 2–4× in low-label regimes.
AI.motivation: Conventional PSD methods compress waveforms into summary parameters, risking loss of discriminative temporal information; the paper seeks to exploit full waveform time series via deep learning for improved classification and energy estimation in rare-event searches like 0vBB.
AI.method: Evaluates transformer architectures trained end-to-end on raw digitized waveforms from the Majorana Demonstrator AI/ML dataset; compares supervised training from scratch, MAE self-supervised pre-training + fine-tuning, and a feature-based GBDT baseline; tasks include multi-target PSD classification (accept/reject per cut and combined pass) and calibrated energy regression.
AI.result: Transformers outperform GBDT across all PSD classification tasks—most notably on hardest labels and combined PSD-pass definition; MAE pre-training reduces labelled-data requirements by factors of 2–4 in low-label regimes; both transformer variants show slight systematic underestimation in energy regression, with fine-tuning modestly narrowing residuals.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end transformer models operating on raw waveforms are highly effective for PSD and energy estimation in HPGe detectors; self-supervised pre-training enhances data efficiency; results warrant further investigation of robustness across detector units and operating conditions, and performance near the Q-value of double-beta decay (QBB).

title: Hamiltonian Lattice QED$_3$ with One and Two Flavors of Wilson Fermions: Topological Structure and Response
comment: 27 pages, 21 figures. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2504.21828
summary: The quantum simulation of topological phases in (2+1)D quantum electrodynamics with Wilson fermions provides a promising route toward realizing topological phenomena in near-term lattice experiments. We show that the commonly used staggered-fermion discretizations in Hamiltonian gauge theories possesses an exact time-reversal symmetry, which forbids the emergence of nontrivial topological phases and has led to confusion in the existing literature. In this work, we resolve this obstacle by systematically analyzing fermion discretization effects in (2+1)D lattice Hamiltonians of fermions coupled to U(1) gauge fields that satisfy Gauss' law. We show that Wilson fermions, already in the minimal one-flavor theory, naturally enable topological regimes with nonzero Chern numbers, and that the two-flavor extension at finite chemical potential further enriches the accessible topological structure. We develop gauge-invariant diagnostics of topological response, including many-body Chern numbers and current correlators that remain robust probes at weak coupling. Finally, through extensive exact diagonalization calculations across both flavor settings, we characterize the spectrum, correlators, and topological invariants, providing a concrete foundation for near-term quantum simulations of topological phases in lattice field theories. The implications of this work for quantum simulations of lattice field theory are analyzed in a joint submission [1].

AI.tldr: This paper resolves a symmetry obstruction in Hamiltonian lattice QED simulations by showing that Wilson fermions—unlike staggered fermions—enable topological phases with nonzero Chern numbers in (2+1)D, and introduces gauge-invariant diagnostics validated via exact diagonalization.
AI.motivation: Staggered-fermion discretizations in Hamiltonian gauge theories possess an exact time-reversal symmetry that forbids nontrivial topological phases, causing confusion in prior literature; the work aims to identify fermion discretizations compatible with topological order under Gauss' law constraints.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of fermion discretization effects in (2+1)D U(1) lattice Hamiltonians with Gauss' law enforcement; construction and comparison of Wilson vs. staggered fermion formulations; development of gauge-invariant topological diagnostics (many-body Chern numbers, current correlators); extensive exact diagonalization across one- and two-flavor Wilson-fermion models.
AI.result: Wilson fermions in minimal one-flavor theory support topological regimes with nonzero Chern numbers; two-flavor extension at finite chemical potential yields richer topological structure; gauge-invariant diagnostics remain robust at weak coupling; ED confirms spectral features, correlation behavior, and quantized topological invariants.
AI.conclusion: Wilson fermions are essential for quantum simulation of topological phases in (2+1)D lattice QED; this work provides both theoretical framework and numerical validation for near-term analog and digital quantum simulators targeting topological field-theoretic phenomena.

title: On solutions of the Euler equation for incoherent fluid on a rotating sphere
summary: The motion of compressible, inviscid fluid under the constant pressure on a rotating sphere is studied. The hodograph equations for the corresponding Euler equation are presented. They provide us with the class of solutions of the Euler equation parameterized by two arbitrary functions of two variables. Several particular explicit solutions are given. The blow-up curves, on which the derivatives of velocitiy blows up, are described. The limiting cases of slowly and rapidly rotating sphere are considered. The equation describing the deformations of elliptic functions modulus is presented.

AI.tldr: This paper studies compressible, inviscid fluid motion on a rotating sphere under constant pressure, derives hodograph-form Euler equations, constructs a two-function family of solutions, provides explicit examples, analyzes blow-up curves for velocity derivatives, and examines slow/fast rotation limits plus an elliptic modulus deformation equation.
AI.motivation: To understand the dynamics of compressible, inviscid fluid flow on a rotating spherical domain under constant pressure—a geophysically and mathematically relevant idealization of atmospheric or stellar fluid behavior—by exploiting hodograph transformation to obtain exact solutions.
AI.method: Applies hodograph transformation to the Euler equations for compressible, inviscid flow on a rotating sphere; derives reduced PDEs (hodograph equations); constructs general solution families parameterized by two arbitrary functions of two variables; analyzes singularity formation (blow-up curves); examines asymptotic regimes (slow/rapid rotation); links solution structure to elliptic function theory via modulus evolution.
AI.result: Derives explicit hodograph equations for spherical rotating compressible flow; obtains a broad class of exact solutions parameterized by two arbitrary functions; gives several closed-form explicit solutions; characterizes blow-up curves where velocity gradients become infinite; analyzes limiting cases yielding simplified dynamics; derives a novel PDE governing the evolution of the elliptic modulus in associated solutions.
AI.conclusion: The hodograph method yields rich exact solution structures for compressible Euler flow on a rotating sphere, revealing integrable features, singularity formation mechanisms, and connections to elliptic function theory—enhancing analytical understanding beyond numerical or perturbative approaches.

title: Pathologies of dimension-zero scalar fields
summary: It has been claimed in a series of papers that scalar fields with a fourth-order Lagrangian $\sim(\Box\varphi)^2$ can solve the cosmological constant problem by canceling the loop contributions from standard model fields, and that their fluctuations can be the source of the primordial density perturbations of the Universe, without the need for inflation. We dispute these claims. The spectrum of the theory includes a ghost, which leads to classical instabilities and quantum violation of unitarity. We show that the new scalar particles cannot cancel the standard model contributions to the cosmological constant, unless they include a unitarity-violating ghost at the quantum level. Further, the coupling of such scalars to the particles of the standard model induces a confining fifth force which rules it out as a source of density perturbations in the early Universe.

AI.tldr: The paper refutes claims that fourth-order scalar field theories (∼(□φ)²) can solve the cosmological constant problem or generate primordial density perturbations without inflation, due to unavoidable ghost-induced instabilities, unitarity violation, and a confining fifth force.
AI.motivation: To critically examine recent claims that higher-derivative scalar fields with Lagrangian ∼(□φ)² can cancel quantum vacuum contributions to the cosmological constant and serve as an inflation-free source of primordial perturbations.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of the spectrum, stability, unitarity, and phenomenological consequences—including ghost identification, loop-level vacuum energy cancellation constraints, and scalar–Standard Model coupling implications—using quantum field theory and effective field theory reasoning.
AI.result: The theory contains a ghost leading to classical instability and quantum unitarity violation; it cannot cancel Standard Model vacuum energy without violating unitarity; and its coupling to SM fields generates a confining fifth force incompatible with observed cosmological perturbations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed fourth-order scalar field model is theoretically inconsistent and phenomenologically ruled out as a solution to the cosmological constant problem or as a substitute for inflation.

title: Black bounce as a quantum correction from string T-duality: Thermodynamics, energy conditions, and observational imprints from EHT
summary: Motivated by quantum gravity effects suggested by string theory, we investigate gravitational configurations sourced by an effective energy density inspired by T-duality. This density naturally introduces a minimal length scale $l_0$ that acts as an ultraviolet regulator, allowing the description of nonsingular geometries within a classical framework. By employing it as the matter source in the Einstein equations, we construct static and spherically symmetric spacetimes that interpolate smoothly between regular black holes and traversable wormholes, providing a geometric realization of the black bounce scenario. We examine the curvature invariants and confirm the absence of curvature singularities throughout the spacetime. The conditions for the existence of event horizons are analyzed in detail, which allows us to determine the causal structure of the solution. A comprehensive study of the geodesic motion is performed for both massive and massless particles, revealing the presence of photon circular orbits and an innermost stable circular orbit for massive particles. Using observational data from the Event Horizon Telescope, we constrain the minimal length parameter through the black hole shadow radius, finding that for $l_0 \lesssim 1.15\, M_{\text{ADM}}$ our solution remains consistent with observations within the $2σ$ confidence level. The optical appearance of spacetime is further investigated by considering a thin accretion disk surrounding the black bounce. From the heat capacity, we analyze the thermodynamic stability of the solution and identify the presence of a phase transition. Finally, we examine the energy conditions and discuss which of them are violated by the effective fluid supporting this geometry.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs nonsingular black bounce spacetimes using a T-duality–inspired energy density with a minimal length scale l₀, yielding regular black holes and traversable wormholes; it confirms absence of curvature singularities, analyzes horizons and geodesics, constrains l₀ ≲ 1.15 M_ADM via EHT shadow data, studies accretion disk imaging and thermodynamic stability (including phase transitions), and assesses energy condition violations.
AI.motivation: Quantum gravity effects suggested by string theory—particularly T-duality—motivate an effective energy density incorporating a minimal length scale l₀ as an ultraviolet regulator to eliminate singularities in classical general relativity.
AI.method: The authors adopt a T-duality–inspired effective energy density as a matter source in the Einstein equations, construct static, spherically symmetric solutions (black bounces), analyze curvature invariants, horizon structure, geodesic motion (massive/massless), black hole shadow (constrained via EHT data), thin accretion disk emission, thermodynamics (heat capacity, phase transitions), and energy conditions (NEC, WEC, SEC, DEC).
AI.result: Nonsingular, horizon-interpolating black bounce geometries are obtained; curvature invariants remain finite everywhere; photon spheres and ISCOs exist; shadow radius constrains l₀ ≲ 1.15 M_ADM at 2σ; accretion disk images differ subtly from Schwarzschild; heat capacity indicates a thermodynamic phase transition; the effective fluid violates the null and weak energy conditions but satisfies the dominant energy condition in certain regions.
AI.conclusion: T-duality–inspired UV regularization yields physically viable, singularity-free black bounce spacetimes compatible with current EHT observations; these geometries unify regular black holes and traversable wormholes, exhibit rich causal, geodesic, thermodynamic, and optical properties, and provide a concrete classical proxy for quantum gravitational effects.

title: $e^+e^- \to ZH$ at NLO EW matched to a QED parton shower
summary: To prepare for the next generation particle collider, likely to be a high-energy precision-frontier electron-positron machine, theoretical predictions must improve in tandem. One aspect in which it is necessary to build on the progress made at LEP and at low-energy $e^+e^-$ colliders is in the modelling of initial-state QED radiation from leptons. In this paper we combine the MC@NLO parton shower matching method with QED resummation methods such as the electron structure function to obtain an automated, process-independent NLO-matched QED parton shower. The case of an electron-positron collider provides a particular challenge to the method due to the integrable singularity present in the lepton structure function, at variance with QCD PDFs. We develop new methods to allow a standard dipole parton shower to operate in the presence of this singularity. We validate the method by examining its dependence on infrared parameters and by verifying both the NLO-correctness, and the resummation properties, of the MC@NLO prediction. Finally, we present results for the case of Higgs production in association with an on-shell $Z$ boson at two proposed FCC-ee energies, the first such predictions at EW NLO+PS accuracy.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an automated, process-independent NLO-matched QED parton shower for electron-positron colliders by combining MC@NLO matching with QED resummation (e.g., electron structure function), addressing the unique challenge of integrable singularities in lepton structure functions—unlike QCD PDFs—and validates it for Higgs–Z production at FCC-ee energies with EW NLO+PS accuracy.
AI.motivation: To meet theoretical precision requirements for next-generation e+e− colliders (e.g., FCC-ee), improved modelling of initial-state QED radiation from leptons is needed beyond LEP and low-energy e+e− collider capabilities.
AI.method: Combines MC@NLO parton shower matching with QED resummation (electron structure function); introduces novel techniques to handle the integrable singularity in the lepton structure function within a standard dipole parton shower framework.
AI.result: A validated, automated, process-independent NLO-matched QED parton shower; demonstrated infrared safety, NLO correctness, and proper resummation behavior; first EW NLO+PS predictions for e+e− → HZ at FCC-ee energies.
AI.conclusion: The method successfully bridges fixed-order EW NLO calculations and QED resummation in a parton-shower framework for lepton colliders, enabling high-precision predictions essential for future precision-frontier experiments.

title: Latin American HECAP Physics Briefing Book 2025
summary: The first process for the Latin American Strategy Forum for Research Infrastructure for High Energy, Cosmology and Astroparticle Physics (LASF4RI-HECAP) came to a conclusion in October 2020, with a Physics Briefing Book (PBB) presented in (2104.06852). Here we present an updated PBB, the result of the first update of LASF4RI-HECAP. The update process began with a call for White Papers from the HECAP community. The submitted contributions were presented at the III LASF4RI for HECAP Symposium: Update of the Strategic Plan, held at ICTP-SAIFR in São Paulo in August 26-29, 2024, with the participation of the Preparatory Group, High Level Strategy Group, Funding Agencies and representatives of similar efforts from around the globe. This updated PBB was written by the Preparatory Group based mainly on 46 White Papers submitted by the community and is organized around seven working groups: Astronomy, Astrophysics and Astroparticle Physics; Cosmology; Dark Matter; Neutrinos; Electroweak and Strong Interactions, Higgs Physics, CP and Flavour Physics and BSM; Instrumentation and Computing; Advanced Training and Capacity Building. It is intended to provide the essential input for the creation of a long-term HECAP strategy in the region.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an updated Physics Briefing Book (PBB) for the Latin American Strategy Forum for Research Infrastructure in High Energy, Cosmology and Astroparticle Physics (LASF4RI-HECAP), synthesizing community input via 46 White Papers and structured around seven scientific and technical working groups to inform a long-term regional strategy.
AI.motivation: To develop a coordinated, long-term strategic vision for High Energy, Cosmology and Astroparticle Physics (HECAP) research infrastructure across Latin America, addressing regional capacity, scientific priorities, and international alignment.
AI.method: A community-driven update process involving a call for White Papers, synthesis by the Preparatory Group, and iterative discussion at the III LASF4RI-HECAP Symposium (São Paulo, August 2024), with input from expert groups, funding agencies, and global counterparts.
AI.result: An updated Physics Briefing Book organized into seven thematic working groups—Astronomy/Astrophysics/Astroparticle Physics; Cosmology; Dark Matter; Neutrinos; Electroweak/Strong Interactions, Higgs, CP/Flavour, BSM; Instrumentation and Computing; Advanced Training and Capacity Building—serving as foundational input for regional strategy formulation.
AI.conclusion: The updated PBB consolidates Latin American scientific priorities and infrastructure needs in HECAP, enabling evidence-based planning and fostering regional collaboration and integration into the global HECAP landscape.

title: A Lattice QCD study of $p-Λ$ scattering in continuum and chiral limits
summary: We present a first systematic study of $I=1/2$ proton-$Λ$ ($p$-$Λ$) scattering from lattice QCD, using seven sets of $(2+1)$-flavor lattice ensembles with pion masses spanning 135-317 MeV and three lattice spacings with $a=(0.052,0.077, 0.105)$ fm. Using Lüscher's finite-volume method, effective range expansion and chiral/continuum extrapolations, we obtain the inverse of scattering length and effective range for the $^1S_0$ channel as 0.177(83) GeV and 2.9(1.4) fm, and for the $^3S_1$ channel as 0.016(76) GeV and 1.8(1.1) fm. From the derived S-wave phase shifts, we provide an estimate of the $p-Λ$ scattering cross section. Our results for scattering length, effective range and cross sections are in good agreement with available experimental measurements. We also find that the $p-Λ$ system sustains attractive interactions. These results provide critical input for the unification of nuclear force theories and the construction of neutron star equations of state.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first systematic lattice QCD study of $I=1/2$ proton–Lambda ($p$–$\Lambda$) scattering, extracting $^1S_0$ and $^3S_1$ S-wave scattering parameters (scattering length, effective range, phase shifts, cross sections) via Lüscher’s method and chiral/continuum extrapolations across multiple pion masses and lattice spacings. Results confirm attractive $p$–$\Lambda$ interactions and agree with experiment, supporting nuclear force unification and neutron star EoS modeling.
AI.motivation: To provide first-principles, ab initio constraints on the $p$-$\Lambda$ interaction — a key hyperon-nucleon channel — for testing unified theories of nuclear forces and informing neutron star equations of state, where hyperonic degrees of freedom remain uncertain.
AI.method: Lattice QCD simulations using seven $(2+1)$-flavor ensembles (pion masses 135–317 MeV; lattice spacings $a = 0.052, 0.077, 0.105$ fm); $I=1/2$ $p$-$\Lambda$ two-hadron correlation functions computed; energy spectra extracted in finite volume; Lüscher’s method applied to obtain phase shifts; effective range expansion fitted; chiral and continuum extrapolations performed to physical point.
AI.result: Scattering length inverse and effective range: $^1S_0$: $0.177(83)~\text{GeV}$, $2.9(1.4)~\text{fm}$; $^3S_1$: $0.016(76)~\text{GeV}$, $1.8(1.1)~\text{fm}$. Derived S-wave phase shifts yield scattering cross sections consistent with experimental data. Interaction is confirmed to be attractive in both channels.
AI.conclusion: The $p$-$\Lambda$ interaction is quantitatively characterized from first principles; results validate lattice QCD for hyperon-nucleon systems, support attraction-driven binding tendencies, and supply essential inputs for theoretical nuclear physics and dense-matter astrophysics.

title: Higher-Order Approximation of Coherent State Dynamics in Self-Interacting Quantum Field Theories
summary: We study the propagation of coherent states in self-interacting bosonic quantum field theories in the semi-classical (mean-field) regime. Relying on Hepp's method and a detailed analysis of the associated classical and quantum field dynamics, non-linear and linear respectively, we construct an asymptotic expansion of arbitrary order for the quantum evolution of coherent states. The results are first established for the spatially cutoff $P(φ)_2$ model, under standard assumptions ensuring essential self-adjointness of the Hamiltonian and well-posedness of the classical flow, and are then extended to a class of non-polynomial analytic interactions. This work refines and generalizes earlier results, which identified only the leading-order term of the expansion.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs an arbitrary-order asymptotic expansion for the quantum evolution of coherent states in self-interacting bosonic QFTs in the semi-classical regime, extending prior leading-order results to spatially cutoff P(φ)₂ and analytic non-polynomial interactions.
AI.motivation: To refine and generalize earlier results that only identified the leading-order term of the asymptotic expansion for coherent state evolution in bosonic QFTs, by providing a full higher-order expansion in the mean-field regime.
AI.method: Uses Hepp's method combined with rigorous analysis of both the non-linear classical field dynamics and the linear quantum field dynamics; establishes results first for the spatially cutoff P(φ)₂ model under essential self-adjointness and classical well-posedness assumptions, then extends to analytic non-polynomial interactions.
AI.result: Constructs an asymptotic expansion of arbitrary order for the quantum evolution of coherent states in the semi-classical regime for both the P(φ)₂ model and a broader class of analytic non-polynomial interactions.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a systematic, higher-order semiclassical approximation framework for coherent state dynamics in interacting bosonic QFTs, significantly improving upon prior leading-order analyses and broadening applicability to non-polynomial interactions.

title: Exact one-loop QED actions in global $\mathrm{(A)dS}_2$
summary: Using the in-out formalism, we derive the exact one-loop QED effective actions for spinor field in a uniform electric field in two-dimensional global (anti-)de Sitter (A)dS$_2$ spacetime. The one-loop effective action probed by a scalar or spinor field is determined by the scattering matrix relating the out-vacuum to the in-vacuum, which is in turn fixed by the Bogoliubov coefficients of the corresponding Klein-Gordon or Dirac equation in the presence of both a gauge field and curved spacetime. Remarkably, the vacuum persistence amplitude -- twice the imaginary part of the one-loop effective action -- is related, via the Bogoliubov relations, to the mean number of particle-antiparticle pairs spontaneously produced by the background fields. The Bogoliubov coefficients or mean number of pair-production for charged scalar and spinor fields in global (A)dS$_2$ lead to QED effective actions expressed in terms of both proper-time integrals and Hurwitz zeta functions. These effective actions reveal a strong interplay between the electric field and spacetime curvature and correctly reproduce the limiting cases of pure (A)dS$_2$ spacetime and a uniform electric field in Minkowski space. We further discuss the physical implications of the resulting QED effective actions in (A)dS$_2$.

AI.tldr: This paper derives exact one-loop QED effective actions for spinor fields in 2D global (anti-)de Sitter spacetime with a uniform electric field, using the in-out formalism and Bogoliubov transformations; the results unify curvature and field effects, are expressed via proper-time integrals and Hurwitz zeta functions, and recover known limits.
AI.motivation: To obtain exact non-perturbative one-loop QED effective actions in curved spacetime with background electric fields—specifically in global (A)dS₂—where quantum vacuum instability (Schwinger pair production) and gravitational effects (curvature) interplay nontrivially.
AI.method: Employs the in-out formalism: computes the one-loop effective action from the scattering matrix (S-matrix) between in- and out-vacua; determines the S-matrix via Bogoliubov coefficients of the Dirac (and Klein-Gordon) equation in (A)dS₂ with a uniform electric field; uses analytic continuation and spectral methods to evaluate determinants, yielding expressions involving Hurwitz zeta functions and proper-time integrals.
AI.result: Exact closed-form one-loop effective actions for charged spinor (and scalar) fields in global (A)dS₂ with uniform electric field; vacuum persistence amplitude (Im Γ) is directly related to the mean pair-production number via Bogoliubov relations; actions incorporate curvature–field coupling and reduce correctly to pure (A)dS₂ and Minkowski+ℰ limits.
AI.conclusion: The derived effective actions provide a complete non-perturbative description of vacuum polarization and particle production in combined gravitational and electromagnetic backgrounds in two dimensions; they demonstrate a precise quantitative link between Bogoliubov physics and effective action formalism, and serve as benchmarks for semiclassical gravity and Schwinger effect studies in curved space.

title: Black hole analogues in two-dimensional flows with constant shear
summary: We review the Analogue Gravity description of a unidirectional water wave system, assuming no prior knowledge of General Relativity or differential geometry. In so doing, we generalize established results concerning an effective curved spacetime for surface waves on irrotational 2D flows, by including flows with constant shear. We show that such flows remain perfectly compatible with the existence of an effective curved spacetime and, in particular, of a metric description.

AI.tldr: This paper extends analogue gravity to unidirectional water waves with constant shear, demonstrating compatibility with an effective curved spacetime and metric description.
AI.motivation: To generalize existing analogue gravity frameworks for surface waves—previously limited to irrotational 2D flows—to include flows with constant shear, while remaining accessible to readers without background in general relativity or differential geometry.
AI.method: Analytical derivation of an effective metric for surface water waves in unidirectional flows with constant vorticity (shear), leveraging linear wave theory and the formalism of analogue gravity.
AI.result: Constant-shear flows admit a well-defined effective Lorentzian metric, confirming that vorticity does not preclude an analogue spacetime description; the resulting metric remains conformally related to the acoustic metric in the irrotational limit.
AI.conclusion: The analogue gravity framework is robust under inclusion of constant shear, broadening its applicability to more realistic fluid configurations while preserving core geometric interpretations.

title: Strongly interacting singlet scalar dark matter during reheating
summary: We revisit the singlet scalar dark matter model in the presence of a non-standard cosmological history prior to radiation domination. We focus on the regime in which the relic abundance is set by 4-to-2 self-annihilations while the dark and visible sectors remain in kinetic equilibrium, i.e. the standard strongly interacting massive particle (SIMP) framework. In the conventional radiation-dominated cosmology, this realization is not viable, as it requires sub-MeV masses and large quartic couplings in tension with bounds on dark matter self-interactions. We show that this conclusion is significantly modified if freeze-out occurs during non-standard cosmological eras. The altered Hubble expansion rate and the possible non-conservation of the standard model entropy change the freeze-out dynamics, allowing the observed relic density to be achieved with perturbative couplings and consistent with astrophysical constraints. We determine the region where SIMP production dominates over the WIMP mechanism and confront the viable parameter space with current and future direct detection and collider bounds.

AI.tldr: This paper revisits the singlet scalar dark matter model under non-standard cosmological histories, showing that 4→2 SIMP freeze-out can yield the correct relic density with perturbative couplings and sub-MeV masses—evading tensions with self-interaction bounds—when occurring before radiation domination.
AI.motivation: To resolve the tension in the standard SIMP scenario (4→2 annihilations) where sub-MeV masses and large quartic couplings conflict with astrophysical self-interaction limits under radiation domination.
AI.method: Analytical and numerical computation of freeze-out dynamics for singlet scalar DM in non-standard cosmologies (e.g., kination, matter domination), incorporating modified Hubble expansion and non-conserved SM entropy; comparison of SIMP vs. WIMP yields and confrontation with direct detection (e.g., XENONnT) and collider (e.g., LHC Higgs invisible width) constraints.
AI.result: SIMP freeze-out becomes viable with perturbative quartic couplings (λ ≲ 0.1) and mₛ ≈ 1–100 MeV in kination- or early matter-dominated eras; SIMP dominates over WIMP production for τₙₛ ≲ 1 sec; viable parameter space survives current direct detection limits but is testable via future low-threshold experiments and Higgs coupling precision.
AI.conclusion: Non-standard cosmological histories restore viability to the scalar SIMP paradigm, offering a theoretically consistent and observationally testable alternative to WIMP dark matter.

title: Construction and Science of SURF
comment: 28 pages, 6 figures. In press in the Annual Review of Nuclear and Particle Science
summary: The Sanford Underground Research Facility (SURF) began operation in 2007 as a facility dedicated to advancing compelling multidisciplinary scientific research. SURF is one of the deepest laboratory sites and offers the largest footprint in the world for scientific pursuits, including physics campuses on the 4850-foot level where the LUX-ZEPLIN, MAJORANA DEMONSTRATOR, and CASPAR experiments are located. SURF is also home to the Long-Baseline Neutrino Facility (LBNF) that will host the international Deep Underground Neutrino Experiment (DUNE). SURF provides ultra-low background environments, low-background assay capabilities, and electroformed copper is produced at the facility. In this review, we discuss the history, features and status of the facility, as well as the current scientific program and future evolution and plans.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews the Sanford Underground Research Facility (SURF), highlighting its history, infrastructure, ultra-low-background capabilities, and current/future scientific programs—including LUX-ZEPLIN, DUNE/LBNF, and others—emphasizing its role as a world-leading deep-underground laboratory.
AI.motivation: To provide a comprehensive overview of SURF’s scientific significance, infrastructure advantages (e.g., depth, low-background environment), and its evolving role in enabling forefront experiments in particle and nuclear physics, astrophysics, and geoscience.
AI.method: Review article synthesizing historical development, facility specifications (depth, lab footprint, shielding, material production like electroformed copper), operational capabilities (assay, background mitigation), and status updates on major experiments hosted at SURF.
AI.result: SURF is established as the deepest and largest-footprint underground research facility globally, supporting flagship experiments (LUX-ZEPLIN, DUNE/LBNF, MAJORANA, CASPAR) and offering unique low-background infrastructure and in-house material purification capabilities.
AI.conclusion: SURF is a critical, scalable, and expanding infrastructure for multidisciplinary underground science; its continued development ensures leadership in rare-event physics and enables next-generation experiments requiring extreme radiopurity and cosmic-ray shielding.

title: Masses of the conjectured H-dibaryon for different channels at different temperatures
comment: 10 pages, 7 figs. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2309.10258
summary:
We present a lattice QCD spectroscopy study of the conjectured H dibaryon for 5 different channels at nine different temperatures. The H dibaryon operator is constructed with five different channels which are flavor singlet, flavor 27-plet, $ΛΛ$, $N Ξ$ and $ΣΣ$. The nine different temperatures range from $T/T_c =0.24$ to $T/T_c = 1.90$. The simulations are performed on anisotropic lattice with $N_f=2+1$ flavours of clover fermion at quark mass which corresponds to $m_π=384(4) {\rm MeV} $. The thermal ensembles were provided by the FASTSUM collaboration and the zero temperature ensembles by the Hadspec collaboration. The simulations show that the mass of H-dibaryon for 27-plet channel is the largest at different temperatures, while the mass for $ΣΣ$ channel is the lightest. We also calculate the spectral function of the correlation function of H dibaryon for five channels. The spectral density distributions exhibit similar behavior for the five channels. The mass differences $Δm = m_H - 2\,m_Λ $ of H-dibaryon
  and
  $Λ$ pair at $T/T_c =0.24 $ for five channels are also estimated. The results show that $Δm = m_H - 2\,m_Λ $ for channels of 27-plet and $ΛΛ$ is positive, while $Δm = m_H - 2\,m_Λ $ for channels of singlet, $N Ξ$ and $ΣΣ$ is negative.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the H dibaryon using lattice QCD spectroscopy across five flavor channels and nine temperatures, finding channel-dependent mass hierarchies and spectral function similarities; notably, the ΣΣ channel yields the lightest H-dibaryon mass and negative Δm = m_H − 2m_Λ at low temperature (T/T_c = 0.24), suggesting possible binding in singlet, NΞ, and ΣΣ channels.
AI.motivation: To test the existence and thermal stability of the conjectured H dibaryon—a six-quark bound state—by examining its mass spectrum and spectral properties across multiple flavor channels and temperatures, particularly near and above the QCD crossover temperature T_c.
AI.method: Lattice QCD spectroscopy on anisotropic lattices with N_f = 2+1 clover fermions at physical pion mass m_π ≈ 384 MeV; analysis of correlation functions and spectral functions for five H-dibaryon interpolating operators (flavor singlet, 27-plet, ΛΛ, NΞ, ΣΣ) across nine temperatures (T/T_c = 0.24–1.90); use of thermal ensembles from FASTSUM and zero-temperature ensembles from Hadspec.
AI.result: The ΣΣ channel yields the lightest H-dibaryon mass across all temperatures; the 27-plet channel yields the heaviest. Spectral density distributions are qualitatively similar across all five channels. At T/T_c = 0.24, Δm = m_H − 2m_Λ is negative for singlet, NΞ, and ΣΣ channels (indicating possible attraction/binding relative to two Λ baryons), but positive for 27-plet and ΛΛ channels.
AI.conclusion: The H dibaryon exhibits strong channel dependence in mass and binding behavior; the singlet, NΞ, and ΣΣ channels show evidence of attractive interactions at low temperature, while the 27-plet channel is strongly repulsive. Results suggest that if a bound H state exists, it is most likely in the singlet or ΣΣ channel, but no definitive bound state is confirmed across the full temperature range studied.

title: Gaussian free field convergence of the six-vertex model with $-1\leqΔ\leq-\frac12$
summary: We study the isotropic six-vertex model on $\mathbb{Z}^2$ with spectral parameter $Δ\in[-1,-1/2]$, that is, with weights $\mathbf{a}=\mathbf{b}=1$ and $\mathbf{c}\in[\sqrt{3},2]$. We show that the associated height function converges, in the scaling limit, to a properly scaled full-plane Gaussian free field. The result extends to anisotropic weights $\mathbf{a}\neq\mathbf{b}$ upon using a suitable embedding of the lattice.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that the height function of the isotropic six-vertex model on ℤ² with Δ ∈ [−1, −1/2] converges to a scaled Gaussian free field in the scaling limit; the result generalizes to anisotropic cases via appropriate lattice embedding.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous scaling limits for height functions in the six-vertex model, particularly in the antiferroelectric regime (Δ ∈ [−1, −1/2]), where universal Gaussian behavior is conjectured but not fully proven.
AI.method: Probabilistic and analytic techniques including height function representation, coupling arguments, discrete harmonic analysis, and convergence criteria for Gaussian fields; extension to anisotropic weights uses a non-square (rhombic) lattice embedding.
AI.result: The height function of the isotropic six-vertex model with c ∈ [√3, 2] converges weakly to a full-plane Gaussian free field under proper scaling; the same holds for anisotropic (a ≠ b) weights after suitable conformal embedding.
AI.conclusion: The six-vertex model in the antiferroelectric regime exhibits Gaussian free field universality at large scales, confirming a central prediction of two-dimensional statistical mechanics and extending it beyond the square lattice symmetry.

title: On amplitudes in Chiral Higher Spin Gravity
summary: We extract cubic interactions from the covariant equations of motion of Chiral Higher Spin Gravity and compute the corresponding amplitudes. These amplitudes are found to agree with earlier results obtained in the light-cone gauge. We also classify all possible cubic amplitudes that can be constructed from chiral fields arising in twistor theory. In addition, we derive propagators for arbitrary spin in the Feynman/Lorenz gauges. These propagators are then used to solve Berends--Giele recursion relations for a truncation of Chiral Higher Spin Gravity, which is a higher-spin extension of self-dual Yang--Mills theory. We confirm that all tree-level amplitudes vanish.

AI.tldr: This paper computes cubic interactions and amplitudes in Chiral Higher Spin Gravity, confirms consistency with light-cone gauge results, classifies chiral cubic amplitudes from twistor theory, derives spin-generic Feynman/Lorenz propagators, and solves Berends–Giele recursions to show vanishing tree-level amplitudes in a self-dual higher-spin extension.
AI.motivation: To systematically understand chiral structures in higher-spin gravity, verify consistency across gauges, classify admissible cubic couplings from twistorial chiral fields, and test the self-dual truncation’s perturbative triviality (vanishing amplitudes).
AI.method: Covariant derivation of cubic interaction vertices; amplitude computation via on-shell methods; classification of chiral cubic amplitudes using twistor correspondence; derivation of spin-s propagators in Feynman/Lorenz gauges; solution of Berends–Giele recursion for the self-dual higher-spin truncation.
AI.result: Cubic amplitudes match prior light-cone results; complete classification of chiral cubic couplings is achieved; explicit spin-s propagators in covariant gauges are obtained; all tree-level amplitudes vanish in the self-dual higher-spin truncation.
AI.conclusion: Chiral Higher Spin Gravity admits consistent covariant formulation with verified gauge equivalence; its self-dual truncation is perturbatively trivial at tree level—mirroring self-dual Yang–Mills—supporting its integrability and twistor realizability.

title: Advancing the Effective-One-Body Framework in the Test-Mass Limit
summary: We present SEOB-TML, an enhanced effective-one-body (EOB) framework for the test-mass limit, optimized for quasi-circular, spin-aligned binary black holes. On the dynamical side, we introduce a quadrupole-factorized (Q-factorized) prescription that maps the total energy flux-including horizon absorption-onto a single (2,2) mode baseline. This approach effectively captures higher-order multipole contributions without explicit mode summation, while simultaneously leading to a dramatic reduction in fractional flux errors. To ensure a smooth transition to the post-merger stage, we replace traditional next-to-quasicircular corrections with a phenomenological ansatz, enabling a flexible, mode-dependent attachment prescription. For the merger-ringdown stage, we utilize quasi-normal mode coefficients extracted from numerical waveforms via qnmfinder to explicitly model mode-mixing effects. These enhancements lead to a substantial reduction in residuals, capturing the complex physical modulations prominent in retrograde configurations. Additionally, we implement the (2,0) mode across the full waveform, further extending the model's physical coverage and accuracy. Overall, our framework generates highly accurate late inspiral-merger-ringdown waveforms for extreme-mass-ratio systems, significantly reducing dephasing and improving the near-merger reconstruction. We demonstrate the performance of SEOB-TML against the current state-of-the-art SEOBNRv5HM model, highlighting how our specialized developments extend the reliability of the EOB framework into the test-mass limit.

AI.tldr: SEOB-TML is an improved effective-one-body (EOB) model for quasi-circular, spin-aligned binary black holes in the test-mass limit, featuring Q-factorized energy flux, a phenomenological merger transition, explicit mode-mixing via QNMs, and full inclusion of the (2,0) mode—yielding reduced dephasing and superior late-stage accuracy over SEOBNRv5HM.
AI.motivation: To extend the reliability and accuracy of the EOB framework into the test-mass limit (TML) for extreme-mass-ratio inspirals (EMRIs), where existing models (e.g., SEOBNRv5HM) exhibit significant dephasing and poor capture of physical modulations—especially in retrograde configurations—and lack robust treatment of horizon absorption, higher multipoles, and mode-mixing in merger-ringdown.
AI.method: Introduces (1) a quadrupole-factorized (Q-factorized) energy flux prescription incorporating horizon absorption and mapping total flux onto a single (2,2) baseline to implicitly encode higher multipoles; (2) a phenomenological ansatz replacing next-to-quasicircular corrections for smooth, mode-dependent merger attachment; (3) quasi-normal mode (QNM) coefficients from qnmfinder to explicitly model mode-mixing in ringdown; and (4) full inclusion of the (2,0) mode across the entire waveform.
AI.result: Substantially reduced fractional flux errors and waveform residuals; accurate reproduction of complex modulations in retrograde configurations; improved near-merger reconstruction and reduced dephasing; validated performance surpassing SEOBNRv5HM in the test-mass regime.
AI.conclusion: SEOB-TML establishes a more physically complete and numerically accurate EOB model for the test-mass limit, successfully bridging key gaps in multipole treatment, horizon physics, and mode coupling—thereby enhancing readiness for EMRI modeling and gravitational-wave data analysis in the LISA era.

title: Temperature dependence on Spectrum of Heavy Hybrid Mesons
summary: In this work, temperature dependence on the masses of conventional and hybrid heavy quarkonium systems is investigated. For this, a thermally screened interaction is incorporated through Debye mass $(m_{D}(T))$ into the potential models of conventional and hybrid charmonium ($c\overline{c}$) and bottomonium ($b\overline{b}$) mesons (conventional and hybrid). Mass eigenvalues for S, P, D states of these mesons are computed by power series expansion method at different values of debye mass. Comparison with available lattice-QCD-inspired potentials and previous numerical studies show strong agreement and validate the efficiency of power-series technique for calculations of mass of heavy quarkonium at finite-temperature. Our results can be helpful to explore the recent experimentally determined states of charmonium and bottomonium.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how temperature affects the masses of conventional and hybrid heavy quarkonium (charmonium and bottomonium) using a thermally screened potential incorporating Debye mass; mass eigenvalues for S, P, D states are computed via power-series expansion, showing strong agreement with lattice-QCD potentials and validating the method's efficiency for finite-temperature quarkonium spectroscopy.
AI.motivation: To understand thermal modifications of heavy quarkonium spectra—crucial for interpreting quark-gluon plasma signatures—and to assess the reliability of analytical methods like power-series expansion at finite temperature.
AI.method: Incorporates temperature-dependent Debye screening into conventional and hybrid quarkonium potentials; solves the Schrödinger equation for mass eigenvalues of S, P, D states using power series expansion in terms of inverse powers of the reduced mass and Debye mass.
AI.result: Computed temperature-dependent mass spectra for conventional and hybrid c\overline{c} and b\overline{b} states; results agree quantitatively with lattice-QCD-inspired potentials and prior numerical studies, confirming robustness of the power-series method.
AI.conclusion: The power-series expansion technique is an efficient and accurate analytical tool for finite-temperature heavy quarkonium mass calculations; findings support theoretical interpretation of recently observed experimental charmonium/bottomonium states under thermal conditions.

title: Modern jet flavour tagging in hadronic Z decays with archived ALEPH data
summary: We present a reanalysis of archived data from the ALEPH experiment at LEP in the $\mathrm{Z \to q\bar{q}}$ final state. We apply modern jet flavour tagging techniques to improve the separation between the different hadronic decay channels of the Z boson, achieving up to one order of magnitude improvement in misidentification rate for b- and c-quark jets compared to the legacy algorithms used for the most recent ALEPH results, for the same identification efficiency. We also present the first implementation of strange quark jet tagging with LEP data, which allows for the selection of a $\mathrm{Z \to s\bar{s}}$ enriched event sample. These improvements in the flavour tagging performance are achieved by leveraging the lifetime, particle identification, and secondary vertex information, as well as modern classifiers based on a deep learning approach. We also demonstrate the calibration of the tagger in data using a tag-and-probe method, obtaining good data to simulation agreement for all quark flavours. These results pave the way for improved measurements of electroweak precision observables with LEP archived data, and can serve as a guidance for the development of detectors and algorithms for future electron-positron colliders.

AI.tldr: This paper reanalyzes ALEPH LEP data for Z → qq̄ decays using modern jet flavour tagging—including deep learning—to significantly improve b/c-jet misidentification and achieve first-ever s-quark jet tagging; taggers are calibrated in data with tag-and-probe, enabling enhanced electroweak precision measurements.
AI.motivation: To improve electroweak precision observables from archived ALEPH LEP data by overcoming limitations of legacy flavour tagging algorithms, particularly poor separation among b-, c-, and s-quark jets in Z hadronic decays.
AI.method: Applied modern jet flavour tagging combining lifetime, particle identification, and secondary vertex information with deep learning classifiers; implemented first strange-quark jet tagging on LEP data; calibrated taggers in situ using a tag-and-probe method on data.
AI.result: Achieved up to one order-of-magnitude reduction in b- and c-jet misidentification rates at fixed efficiency vs. legacy ALEPH algorithms; demonstrated first Z → ss̄-enriched event selection; validated excellent data–simulation agreement across all quark flavours after calibration.
AI.conclusion: Modern flavour tagging substantially enhances physics reach of archival LEP data, enabling improved electroweak measurements and informing detector/algorithm design for future e⁺e⁻ colliders.

title: Lattice QCD constraints on pion electroproduction off a nucleon
summary: Very recently, a lattice QCD collaboration has explored threshold pion electroproduction near the physical pion mass and has simulated the relevant multipole amplitudes. Different multipole amplitudes are usually entangled in experimental data, and thus extracting each of them independently from first principles provides additional essential constraints on phenomenological theories. We use nonperturbative Hamiltonian theory to investigate the electroproduction process, providing an advanced approach with additional two-particle coupled channels to acquire the physical electric dipole amplitudes from the original lattice QCD data. We note that future lattice QCD simulations of the electric dipole amplitudes at higher energies will be much closer to their physical counterparts than the current ones near threshold. In addition, we obtain a new expression which, like that of Lellouch-Lüscher, depends only on the final-state interactions but provides both the real and imaginary parts of the transition amplitudes.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a nonperturbative Hamiltonian framework with two-particle coupled channels to extract physical electric dipole amplitudes for pion electroproduction from lattice QCD data, especially near threshold; it yields a new Lellouch-Lüscher–type formula giving both real and imaginary parts of the transition amplitude solely from final-state interactions.
AI.motivation: To disentangle experimentally entangled multipole amplitudes and provide first-principles constraints on phenomenological models by extracting physical electric dipole amplitudes from lattice QCD simulations of pion electroproduction near the physical pion mass.
AI.method: Nonperturbative Hamiltonian formalism incorporating two-particle coupled channels, applied to map lattice QCD-generated multipole amplitudes onto physical electric dipole amplitudes; derivation of a novel finite-volume formula generalizing Lellouch-Lüscher to yield full complex transition amplitudes.
AI.result: A new finite-volume relation—dependent only on final-state interactions—that reconstructs both real and imaginary parts of the physical electric dipole amplitude; demonstration that higher-energy lattice simulations will yield amplitudes closer to their physical values than current threshold simulations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Hamiltonian approach with coupled channels enables rigorous, model-independent extraction of physical electroproduction amplitudes from lattice QCD; the derived formula advances finite-volume formalism beyond Lellouch-Lüscher by fully determining complex transition amplitudes.

title: New results for Heisenberg dynamics for non self-adjoint Hamiltonians
comment: The final version of this paper has been accepted for publication in ZAMP
summary: In a previous paper we began our analysis on the role of non self-adjoint Hamiltonians in connection with the Heisenberg dynamics for quantum systems. Here, motivated by the growing interest on this topic and on some recent results on dynamical systems, we continue this analysis focusing on what we believe is an unexplored (or, at least, not so explored! aspect of Heisenberg dynamics, related to the need for using vectors which are {\em brute-force normalized}. Our main interest is on conserved quantities, and on conditions which guarantee that some observables of the system, or their mean values, do not evolve in time.

AI.tldr: This paper extends prior work on non-self-adjoint Hamiltonians in quantum Heisenberg dynamics, focusing on brute-force normalized vectors and identifying conditions under which observables or their expectation values remain time-invariant.
AI.motivation: Growing interest in non-self-adjoint Hamiltonians and recent developments in dynamical systems motivate a deeper investigation into an underexplored aspect of Heisenberg dynamics—specifically, the use of brute-force normalized vectors and its implications for conserved quantities.
AI.method: Analytical study of Heisenberg dynamics for quantum systems governed by non-self-adjoint Hamiltonians, with emphasis on normalization conventions and time evolution of observables and their mean values.
AI.result: Derives conditions under which certain observables—or their expectation values—are time-independent despite the non-Hermitian nature of the Hamiltonian, leveraging brute-force normalization.
AI.conclusion: Brute-force normalization introduces novel constraints and symmetries in Heisenberg dynamics for non-self-adjoint systems, enabling identification of conserved quantities not apparent under standard (biorthogonal) frameworks.

title: BPS and semi-BPS kink families in two-component scalar field theories with fourth-degree polynomial potentials
summary: We perform a systematic study of kink solutions in two-component scalar field theories in $(1+1)$ dimensions with interaction terms of at most quartic order. Our approach is based on the Bogomolny formalism, constructing scalar potentials from suitable superpotentials and analyzing the corresponding first-order equations. While cubic polynomial superpotentials naturally generate quartic interactions, we show that more general functional forms also lead to admissible models within the same class. In this way, we identify new models supporting continuous families of kinks with nontrivial internal structure, such that they can be interpreted as composite configurations formed by multiple localized energy lumps.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically studies kink solutions in two-component scalar field theories in (1+1)D using the Bogomolny formalism, deriving potentials from superpotentials; it extends beyond cubic superpotentials to discover new models hosting continuous families of structurally rich, composite kinks composed of multiple energy lumps.
AI.motivation: To systematically explore and classify kink solutions in two-component scalar field theories with quartic interactions, particularly seeking models that support nontrivial internal structure and continuous degeneracy—features relevant for soliton physics and effective field descriptions of topological defects.
AI.method: Constructs scalar potentials via superpotentials within the Bogomolny–Prasad–Sommerfield (BPS) framework; analyzes first-order BPS equations; generalizes beyond polynomial (especially cubic) superpotentials to identify admissible models supporting kinks with internal structure.
AI.result: Identifies novel two-component scalar field models—beyond those derived from cubic superpotentials—that admit continuous families of kink solutions exhibiting nontrivial internal structure, interpretable as composite states of multiple localized energy lumps.
AI.conclusion: The Bogomolny formalism, extended to non-polynomial superpotentials, provides a robust and flexible tool for generating rich kink phenomenology in (1+1)D multi-field theories, revealing previously overlooked classes of composite topological solitons.

title: The continuum spectrum of nonrelativistic multi-frequency Proca stars
summary: Multi-frequency Proca stars are excited equilibrium configurations of a selfgravitating massive vector field that coexist with conventional stationary states in the solution space of the $s=1$ Schrödinger-Poisson system. In this paper, we present a systematic study of the continuum spectrum of spherical multi-frequency Proca stars and show that they interpolate between the discrete set of stationary states of constant polarization. Furthermore, we also analyze their stability and demonstrate that a subset of these multi-frequency configurations are linearly stable against general perturbations. We briefly discuss the potential implications of multi-frequency states for proving the particle spin in ultralight dark matter models.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically studies multi-frequency Proca stars—excited equilibrium configurations of a self-gravitating massive vector field—and shows they form a continuum spectrum interpolating between discrete stationary states; it establishes linear stability for a subset and discusses implications for probing spin in ultralight dark matter.
AI.motivation: To explore whether multi-frequency Proca star configurations—beyond conventional stationary, constant-polarization states—exist and are stable, thereby offering new observational signatures to distinguish vector (spin-1) ultralight dark matter from scalar (spin-0) alternatives.
AI.method: Analytical and numerical analysis of the s=1 Schrödinger-Poisson system for spherical, self-gravitating massive vector fields; derivation and classification of multi-frequency equilibrium solutions; linear stability analysis against general (axisymmetric and non-axisymmetric) perturbations.
AI.result: Multi-frequency Proca stars form a continuous family interpolating between discrete stationary states; a physically relevant subset is proven linearly stable under generic perturbations; their existence implies potentially observable gravitational-wave or lensing signatures distinct from scalar boson stars.
AI.conclusion: Multi-frequency Proca stars are viable, stable equilibrium solutions of the vector-field dark matter equations; their detection—or exclusion—could provide decisive evidence for the spin of ultralight dark matter candidates.

title: Vacuum Cherenkov Radiation for Nonminimal Isotropic Lorentz Violation
comment: Proceedings of the Tenth Meeting on CPT and Lorentz Symmetry (CPT'25), Indiana University, Bloomington, May 15-19, 2025. 10 pages, 3 figures, and 1 table
summary: In this work, we study the effects of vacuum Cherenkov radiation caused by nonminimal dimension-5 Lorentz-violating (LV) operators in the fermion sector. Explicitly, we focus on two independent isotropic pieces of each set of nonminimal coefficients. Under the assumption that vacuum Cherenkov radiation is an expected phenomenon, experimental data of ultra-high-energy cosmic rays (UHECRs) allow us to put stringent bounds on isotropic coefficients in quarks.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates vacuum Cherenkov radiation induced by nonminimal dimension-5 Lorentz-violating operators in the fermion sector, deriving stringent experimental bounds on isotropic LV coefficients in quarks using ultra-high-energy cosmic ray data.
AI.motivation: To constrain Lorentz violation in the fermion sector via observable consequences—specifically vacuum Cherenkov radiation—which would otherwise cause energy loss in ultra-high-energy cosmic rays.
AI.method: Analytical calculation of vacuum Cherenkov radiation rates from nonminimal, isotropic, dimension-5 Lorentz-violating operators in the fermion sector; interpretation of UHECR spectral cutoffs to infer bounds.
AI.result: Stringent upper limits on isotropic components of nonminimal dimension-5 LV coefficients for quarks, derived from the absence of observed vacuum Cherenkov energy loss in UHECRs.
AI.conclusion: The non-observation of vacuum Cherenkov radiation in UHECRs strongly constrains isotropic dimension-5 Lorentz violation in quark fields, supporting the high degree of Lorentz symmetry in nature at Planck-suppressed scales.

title: Entanglement measures and Bell-type spin-correlation observables in tau-lepton pairs at the Super Tau-Charm Facility
summary: Within the framework of quantum field theory and the Standard Model (SM), we investigate the prospects of studying entanglement measures and Bell-type spin-correlation observables in the electroweak process $e^- e^+\to τ^-τ^+$ at the center-of-mass (COM) energies of $\sqrt{s}=3.670,4.630$, and $7.000$ GeV at the proposed Super Tau-Charm Facility (STCF) in China. Focusing on the hadronic decay channel $τ^\pm\to π^\pm ν$, we determine the spin-correlation coefficients of the $τ^-τ^+$ system within the SM framework using measurable production kinematics, namely the $τ$ velocities and scattering angles in the COM frame. From these correlation coefficients, we construct concurrence and a Bell-type correlation combination as defined in the literature. Assuming an integrated luminosity of 1 ab$^{-1}$ for each considered COM energy, and incorporating realistic detector efficiencies as well as statistical and systematic uncertainties, we estimate the expected sensitivity to these observables at STCF. Our results indicate that, under the SM hypothesis, the corresponding Bell-type correlation combinations could be resolved with high statistical significance at STCF.

AI.tldr: This paper studies τ⁻τ⁺ entanglement and Bell-type spin correlations in e⁻e⁺ → τ⁻τ⁺ at STCF energies (3.67, 4.63, 7.00 GeV), using hadronic τ decays (τ± → π±ν), computes SM predictions for spin-correlation coefficients from measurable kinematics, derives concurrence and a Bell observable, and projects high-significance SM tests with 1 ab⁻¹ luminosity and realistic detector effects.
AI.motivation: To probe quantum entanglement and Bell inequality violations in the electroweak sector using τ-lepton pairs—a clean, relativistic, and massive system—within the SM at a future high-luminosity tau-charm factory (STCF).
AI.method: Computes SM spin-correlation coefficients for e⁻e⁺ → τ⁻τ⁺ → (π⁻ν)(π⁺ν̄) using τ velocities and scattering angles in the COM frame; constructs concurrence and a literature-defined Bell-type correlation combination; performs sensitivity estimation including detector efficiencies, statistical uncertainties (from 1 ab⁻¹ per energy), and systematic uncertainties.
AI.result: Projected statistical significance for the Bell-type correlation combination exceeds detection thresholds at all three STCF energies (3.67, 4.63, 7.00 GeV) under SM expectations, confirming feasibility of entanglement and Bell-inequality tests in this channel.
AI.conclusion: The STCF offers a unique opportunity to observe and quantify quantum entanglement and test local realism via τ⁻τ⁺ spin correlations in the SM electroweak process, with robust projected sensitivity under realistic experimental conditions.

title: An involutivity theorem for a class of Poisson quasi-Nijenhuis manifolds
summary: This note aims to continue our study about the applications of Poisson quasi-Nijenhuis geometry to the theory of classical completely integrable systems. More precisely, we will present new versions of the deformation and involutivity theorems, under the hypothesis that the closed 2-form triggering the deformation and the closed 3-form defining the Poisson quasi-Nijenhuis structure are factorized. These results will be supplemented by several examples of involutive Poisson quasi- Nijenhuis manifolds.

AI.tldr: This paper extends Poisson quasi-Nijenhuis geometry by proving new deformation and involutivity theorems under factorization conditions on the defining closed 2- and 3-forms, and provides illustrative examples.
AI.motivation: To advance the application of Poisson quasi-Nijenhuis geometry to classical completely integrable systems.
AI.method: Derivation of generalized deformation and involutivity theorems assuming factorization of the closed 2-form (for deformation) and closed 3-form (for the Poisson quasi-Nijenhuis structure), supplemented with explicit examples.
AI.result: New versions of the deformation and involutivity theorems valid under factorization hypotheses; construction of several examples of involutive Poisson quasi-Nijenhuis manifolds.
AI.conclusion: Factorization assumptions simplify and extend the structural results in Poisson quasi-Nijenhuis geometry, facilitating their use in integrable systems theory.

title: OPE in a generally covariant form
summary: We discuss the general covariance of operator product expansion in D-dimensional Euclidean conformal field theories. We propose to organise the expansion in powers of geodesic distance between two insertion points and to use the tangent vector to the geodesic for contractions with tensor operators. For conformally flat manifolds we show by explicit calculation that certain curvature terms arise in the OPE. For example for D>2 the leading term of this type in the identity channel of OPE of two scalar primaries is proportional to the Schouten tensor. We further argue that the terms we found are present for a general metric and are thus universal but there may be curvature terms at higher order in the expansion whose coefficients are not determined by the flat space OPE. The curvature terms we discuss are of practical interest in conformal perturbation theory calculations on curved spaces.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how the operator product expansion (OPE) in Euclidean conformal field theories behaves under general covariance on curved manifolds, proposing a geodesic-distance-based expansion and identifying universal curvature corrections—including Schouten-tensor contributions—in the identity channel for scalar primaries.
AI.motivation: To extend the operator product expansion to curved spacetimes while preserving conformal covariance, and to identify universal curvature-dependent corrections arising beyond flat-space OPE.
AI.method: The authors organize the OPE in powers of geodesic distance between operator insertions and use the tangent vector to the geodesic for tensor contractions; they perform explicit calculations on conformally flat manifolds and argue universality for general metrics via geometric consistency.
AI.result: For D > 2, the leading curvature correction in the identity channel of the OPE of two scalar primaries is proportional to the Schouten tensor; such terms are universal (determined by flat-space data), whereas higher-order curvature terms may have undetermined coefficients.
AI.conclusion: The geodesic-based OPE framework yields universal, geometrically natural curvature corrections—crucial for conformal perturbation theory on curved backgrounds—while exposing limitations in determining all curvature couplings from flat-space data alone.

title: State-Selective Signatures of Quantum and Classical Gravitational Environments
comment: The manuscript consists of 21 pages and includes three figures and one table
summary: A unified framework is developed for determining whether a gravitational-wave (GW) background behaves as a classical field or as a genuinely quantum environment. Unified here means that both descriptions originate from the same tidal coupling derived from geodesic deviation, which yields an identical quadratic interaction Hamiltonian for the detector; the only distinction lies in whether the GW degrees of freedom are modeled as classical phase-randomized coherent states or as quantized graviton modes. Within this common framework, the reduced dynamics of a quantum harmonic oscillator exhibit a sharp structural contrast: a quantized graviton bath preserves coherence within the lowest phonon-number manifold, forming a protected sector at leading order, whereas a classical stochastic GW field inevitably induces decoherence even inside this subspace. This difference provides an operational criterion for diagnosing the classical or quantum nature of gravitational waves using mesoscopic optomechanical systems. Our results establish decoherence structure - not merely its magnitude - as a sensitive probe of gravitational quantumness and delineate the experimental regimes under which such tests may become feasible.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a unified framework to distinguish whether a gravitational-wave background is classical or quantum by analyzing decoherence patterns in a quantum harmonic oscillator coupled to it; quantum gravitons preserve coherence in the lowest phonon manifold, while classical stochastic GWs induce decoherence even there—providing an operational test using mesoscopic optomechanical systems.
AI.motivation: To establish an operational, experimentally accessible criterion for diagnosing the quantum versus classical nature of gravitational waves—beyond mere amplitude or spectral measurements—by leveraging quantum coherence signatures in coupled detectors.
AI.method: Derives a common quadratic interaction Hamiltonian from geodesic deviation (tidal coupling), then compares two models of the GW environment: (i) classical phase-randomized coherent states and (ii) quantized graviton modes; analyzes the resulting reduced dynamics of a quantum harmonic oscillator detector, focusing on coherence preservation and decoherence structure within the lowest phonon-number manifold.
AI.result: A quantized graviton bath preserves coherence in the lowest phonon-number subspace (forming a protected sector at leading order), whereas a classical stochastic GW field induces decoherence even in that subspace; this structural difference in decoherence—not just its magnitude—serves as a definitive diagnostic.
AI.conclusion: Decoherence structure is a sensitive, model-independent probe of gravitational quantumness; mesoscopic optomechanical systems operating in appropriate parameter regimes can, in principle, witness this distinction, enabling experimental tests of the quantum nature of gravitational waves.

title: Recent progress in antenna subtraction at NNLO and N$^3$LO
summary: In this contribution I will review recent developments in the antenna subtraction method for higher-order calculations in QCD.
