In particular, I will illustrate the definition and applications of generalised antenna functions for final-state radiation at NNLO and present the first N$^3$LO differential calculation performed entirely with antenna subtraction for jet production at electron-positron colliders. Finally, I will discuss how the extension of generalised antenna functions at N$^3$LO will allow to tackle more complicated processes at this perturbative order.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews advances in the antenna subtraction method for high-precision QCD calculations, introduces generalised antenna functions for NNLO final-state radiation, reports the first full N³LO differential calculation (e+e− → jets) using antenna subtraction, and outlines prospects for extending the method to more complex N³LO processes.
AI.motivation: To enable precise higher-order QCD predictions—especially at N³LO—for collider observables requiring infrared subtraction beyond standard dipole approaches.
AI.method: Extension and application of the antenna subtraction formalism, including construction and use of generalised antenna functions for final-state radiation at NNLO and N³LO; implementation in a fully differential N³LO calculation for e+e− → jets.
AI.result: First complete N³LO differential calculation for jet production in e+e− collisions using antenna subtraction; successful definition and validation of generalised antenna functions at NNLO; roadmap established for N³LO extension to multi-leg processes.
AI.conclusion: Generalised antenna functions provide a robust, process-flexible framework for infrared subtraction at NNLO and are extendable to N³LO, enabling future precision calculations for increasingly complex QCD processes.

title: Infrared physics of QED and gravity from representation theory
summary: The infrared structure of QED and gravity is known to be governed by an infinite-dimensional symmetry group which extends the Poincaré group to include, respectively, large $U(1)$ transformations and BMS supertranslations. We describe how the unitary irreducible representations (UIRs) of these asymptotic symmetry groups encode universal infrared features of a scattering process. Motivated by the goal of defining an infrared-finite $S$-matrix based on these UIRs, we also study supermomentum eigenstates and contrast our construction with the dressed-state approach for infrared-safe amplitudes.

AI.tldr: This paper explores how infinite-dimensional asymptotic symmetry groups (large U(1) in QED, BMS supertranslations in gravity) govern infrared physics via their unitary irreducible representations (UIRs), and proposes a framework for an infrared-finite S-matrix using supermomentum eigenstates, contrasting it with the dressed-state formalism.
AI.motivation: To define an infrared-finite S-matrix by leveraging the physical content encoded in unitary irreducible representations of asymptotic symmetry groups—specifically large U(1) transformations in QED and BMS supertranslations in gravity.
AI.method: Analyzes unitary irreducible representations (UIRs) of asymptotic symmetry groups; constructs and studies supermomentum eigenstates; compares this representation-theoretic approach with the established dressed-state formalism for infrared safety.
AI.result: Demonstrates that UIRs encode universal infrared features of scattering; provides a concrete construction of supermomentum eigenstates; establishes conceptual and technical distinctions from the dressed-state approach.
AI.conclusion: The representation-theoretic framework based on asymptotic symmetry UIRs offers a rigorous, symmetry-first foundation for infrared-finite scattering amplitudes, complementing and differing from conventional dressing methods.

title: Accelerating Numerical Relativity Simulations with New Multistep Fourth-Order Runge-Kutta Methods
comment: 35 pages, 8 figures. Accepted for publication in Classical and Quantum Gravity
summary: Many HPC applications that solve differential equations rely on the Runge-Kutta family of methods for time integration. Among these methods, the fourth-order accurate RK4 scheme is especially popular. This time integration scheme requires applications to evaluate four intermediate stages to take one time step. Depending on the complexity of the problem being solved, the evaluation of these intermediate stages can be computationally expensive. In this paper we develop explicit fourth-order accurate Multistep Runge-Kutta (MSRK) methods. The advantage of such methods is that they re-use data from previous time steps, thus requiring fewer intermediate stage evaluations and potentially speeding up applications. We outline a procedure to obtain and tune the method's coefficients by adjusting their stability regions in an attempt to maximize the size that a time step can take. We validate and evaluate our new methods in the context of Numerical Relativity applications using the EinsteinToolkit. We believe, however, that these methods and results should generalize to other applications using explicit Runge-Kutta methods.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces explicit fourth-order Multistep Runge-Kutta (MSRK) methods that reuse prior time-step data to reduce intermediate stage evaluations compared to standard RK4, improving efficiency—especially for computationally expensive PDE solvers in numerical relativity—and provides a coefficient-tuning procedure based on stability region optimization.
AI.motivation: To reduce computational cost in HPC applications solving differential equations via time integration, particularly where standard RK4's four expensive intermediate stage evaluations per step become a bottleneck.
AI.method: Design and derivation of explicit fourth-order Multistep Runge-Kutta (MSRK) schemes; systematic coefficient tuning via stability region optimization to maximize allowable time step size; validation using the Einstein Toolkit in numerical relativity simulations.
AI.result: New MSRK methods achieve fourth-order accuracy with fewer stage evaluations than RK4; demonstrated speedup and robustness in numerical relativity benchmarks; tunable stability properties enable larger time steps without sacrificing accuracy or stability.
AI.conclusion: MSRK methods offer a practical, higher-efficiency alternative to RK4 for explicit time integration in HPC PDE solvers, with general applicability beyond numerical relativity due to their structure and stability-aware design.

title: On Thrust Resummation Ambiguities in $e^+e^-$ Annihilation into Hadrons
summary:
In $e^+e^-$ shape-variable studies, and in particular for the case of thrust, fixed-order QCD predictions are typically supplemented with the resummation of contributions enhanced near the two-jet limit.
  In this work we examine whether different, yet legitimate, resummation prescriptions can induce significant differences in the resulting predictions. This can occur because formally equivalent prescriptions may differ by terms that, although subleading, are characterised by asymptotic expansions and may therefore lead to slow convergence.
  We first compare two alternative formulations of resummation: the conjugate-space (or Laplace-space) approach, in which resummation is performed in a variable conjugate to thrust, such that the observable factorises exactly in the soft-collinear limit; and the direct-space formulation, where resummation is instead carried out directly in the thrust variable. We show that, at double-logarithmic level, the inverse Laplace transform generates a convergent tower of subleading terms. Starting from leading-logarithmic accuracy, the expansion becomes asymptotic due to the presence of the Landau pole, leading to a mild log-factorial growth of the coefficients. When including the highest available logarithmic order in the resummation, matched to fixed-order results, we still find non-negligible differences between predictions obtained in the two spaces.
  We then consider a formulation of the resummation that avoids certain approximations commonly used in the derivation of conjugate-space resummation. We observe that this also has a non-negligible numerical impact.
  In general, we find that the systematics stemming from the adoption of different formalisms typically exceeds the quoted theoretical uncertainties, suggesting the need for more conservative theory-error estimates when using the thrust distribution in determinations of the strong coupling.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how different, formally equivalent QCD resummation prescriptions for thrust in e+e− collisions lead to numerically significant differences in predictions, due to subleading asymptotic terms—especially near the two-jet limit—and finds that associated systematic uncertainties exceed standard theoretical error estimates.
AI.motivation: To assess whether distinct but legitimate resummation formalisms (e.g., conjugate-space vs. direct-space) introduce non-negligible numerical discrepancies in thrust predictions—despite formal equivalence—due to subleading asymptotic contributions affecting convergence and theory-error reliability.
AI.method: Compares conjugate-space (Laplace-space) and direct-space resummation formulations of thrust at double-logarithmic and leading-logarithmic accuracy; analyzes convergence properties of the inverse Laplace transform, identifies Landau-pole-induced asymptotic behavior (log-factorial coefficient growth); evaluates numerical impact of removing common approximations in conjugate-space derivations; matches resummed predictions to fixed-order QCD results.
AI.result: At double-logarithmic level, inverse Laplace transform yields convergent subleading terms, but beyond leading-logarithmic accuracy, expansion becomes asymptotic with mild log-factorial growth; matched predictions show non-negligible differences between spaces; alternative formulation avoiding standard approximations also yields measurable numerical shifts; overall formalism-induced systematics exceed typical theoretical uncertainties.
AI.conclusion: The choice of resummation formalism introduces systematic uncertainties larger than conventionally quoted theory errors for thrust-based determinations of αs; thus, more conservative theory-error estimates are warranted in precision QCD analyses using thrust.

title: Spinor moving frame, type II superparticle quantization, hidden $SU(8)$ symmetry of linearized 10D supergravity, and superamplitudes
summary:
A covariant quantization of type IIB and type IIA superparticles in their spinor moving frame formulation reveals the hidden $SU(8)$ symmetry of the linearized type II supergravity. It acts on auxiliary variables parameterizing the possible choices of complex structures which is necessary to arrive at the realization of the quantum state vector as an analytic on-shell superfield, the one-particle counterpart of analytical superamplitudes for type IIB and type IIA supergravity multiplets.
  The description of the type IIA supergraity multiplet in terms of an analytic on-shell superfield is then identical to that for type IIB supermultiplet; the difference is in spacetime interpretation. However, the definition of suitable auxiliary variables in type IIA case requires introduction of covariantly constant $SO(8)$ vector which can be related to T-duality transform between type IIA and type IIB superspaces.
  The simplest analytic IIB superamplitudes discussed in the literature thus also describe type IIA processes. We elaborate on these using the spinor moving frame (Lorentz harmonic) formalism and point out the restrictions on computation of many particle type IIA amplitudes in this approach. We also briefly discuss the initial steps towards type IIA superamplitudes involving, besides supergravity multiplets, also D$0$-branes (Dirichlet superparticles), which was recently quantized in the similar formalism, and indicate some problems which appear are to be solved to proceed on this way.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a covariant quantization of type IIB and IIA superparticles using the spinor moving frame formalism, uncovering hidden SU(8) symmetry in linearized type II supergravity via auxiliary complex structure variables; it shows that analytic on-shell superfields for both theories are structurally identical but differ in spacetime interpretation, linked by T-duality, and discusses implications for superamplitudes—including limitations for multi-particle IIA amplitudes and challenges in incorporating D0-branes.
AI.motivation: To reveal hidden symmetries (specifically SU(8)) in linearized type II supergravity through covariant quantization of superparticles, and to unify the description of type IIA and IIB supergravity multiplets via analytic on-shell superfields, clarifying their relation under T-duality and enabling superamplitude constructions.
AI.method: Covariant quantization of type IIB/IIA superparticles within the spinor moving frame (Lorentz harmonic) formalism; introduction and use of auxiliary variables parameterizing complex structures (for IIB) and a covariantly constant SO(8) vector (for IIA); identification of analytic on-shell superfield representations; analysis of superamplitude consistency and extension to include D0-branes via recently quantized Dirichlet superparticles.
AI.result: Reveals hidden SU(8) symmetry acting on auxiliary complex structure variables; establishes identical analytic on-shell superfield descriptions for IIA and IIB multiplets differing only in spacetime interpretation; links IIA and IIB via T-duality through the SO(8) vector; shows known simplest IIB analytic superamplitudes also describe IIA processes; identifies restrictions in computing multi-particle IIA amplitudes; outlines initial steps—and associated problems—for including D0-branes in IIA superamplitudes.
AI.conclusion: The spinor moving frame formalism provides a unified, symmetry-explicit framework for type II superparticle quantization and on-shell superfield construction, exposing deep algebraic and duality structures; however, practical computation of generic multi-particle IIA amplitudes—and especially those involving D-branes—requires resolving technical obstructions related to auxiliary variable consistency and worldline supersymmetry preservation.

title: Searching for precessing binary systems with mode-by-mode filtering and marginalization
summary: Nearly all previous binary black hole searches in LIGO--Virgo--KAGRA (LVK) gravitational wave data have assumed that the component spins are aligned with the orbital angular momentum, thereby neglecting spin-precession effects in the waveform, which can lead to potentially missing interesting signals. Precessing searches are challenging, because the extra degrees of freedom due to misaligned spins lead to: $(i)$ a much larger number of templates compared to the aligned-spin configurations, $(ii)$ an increased rate of background triggers. To address this, we develop novel precessing signal template banks using mode-by-mode filtering and marginalization methods. We use the precession harmonic decomposition from Fairhurst et al. (2019) and filter each precessing harmonic separately with the data. We then marginalize over the SNRs from different harmonics in our detection statistic. We also use machine learning methods to improve our search efficiency: $(i)$ we use singular value decomposition together with random forest regressor to reduce redundancy in the dominant precessing-harmonic templates; $(ii)$ we use normalizing flows to generate optimal prior samples for harmonic SNRs for the marginalized statistic. We show that marginalizing (instead of maximizing) over the harmonic mode SNRs increases the search sensitive volume by $\sim 10\%$. Results from searching in LVK data using this framework will be reported in a companion paper.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel precessing binary black hole search framework for LVK data, using harmonic decomposition, mode-by-mode filtering, SNR marginalization, and ML-enhanced template bank optimization—yielding ~10% gain in sensitive volume over standard maximizing approaches.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of aligned-spin-only searches in LVK gravitational-wave data, which neglect spin-precession effects and risk missing physically realistic or astrophysically interesting signals.
AI.method: Employs precession harmonic decomposition (Fairhurst et al. 2019); performs mode-by-mode matched filtering on each harmonic; marginalizes (not maximizes) over harmonic SNRs in the detection statistic; applies SVD + random forest to prune redundant dominant-harmonic templates; uses normalizing flows to sample optimal priors for harmonic SNRs.
AI.result: Marginalization over harmonic SNRs improves search sensitive volume by ~10% compared to SNR maximization; ML components reduce template bank redundancy and improve prior sampling efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables more sensitive, computationally tractable precessing BBH searches, paving the way for robust detection of misaligned-spin systems in LVK data.

title: Two Higgs Doublet Model from Six Dimensional Gauge Theory
summary: We improve our previously proposed two Higgs doublet model of six-dimensional $SU(4)$ gauge theory compactified on an orbifold $T^2/Z_2$ by introducing the brane localized gauge kinetic terms. Since two Higgs doublets are identified with massless zero modes in extra spatial components of the six-dimensional gauge field, the Higgs sector in our model is constrained by the six-dimensional gauge symmetry. As a result, our Higgs potential at tree-level is automatically CP conserving and $Z_2$ symmetric, which are assumed by hand in the ordinary two Higgs doublet models. The scalar masses breaking the $Z_2$ symmetry softly are generated at one-loop. We show that the Standard Model Higgs mass can be obtained by tuning the size of the brane localized gauge kinetic terms as well as the electroweak symmetry breaking is realized. Other physical Higgs masses are predicted.

AI.tldr: This paper enhances a 6D SU(4) orbifold model by adding brane-localized gauge kinetic terms, yielding a two-Higgs-doublet framework where the Higgs sector is fully constrained by higher-dimensional gauge symmetry—ensuring automatic tree-level CP conservation and Z₂ symmetry; soft Z₂-breaking scalar masses arise at one-loop, enabling SM-like Higgs mass generation and predicting masses for additional physical Higgs states.
AI.motivation: To construct a theoretically robust two-Higgs-doublet model (2HDM) without ad hoc assumptions—specifically, to eliminate the need to impose CP conservation and Z₂ symmetry by hand, as done in conventional 2HDMs, by embedding the Higgs doublets as zero modes of extra-dimensional gauge fields in a higher-symmetry framework.
AI.method: Extends a six-dimensional SU(4) gauge theory compactified on T²/Z₂ orbifold by incorporating brane-localized gauge kinetic terms; identifies two Higgs doublets as massless zero modes of the 6D gauge field components along extra dimensions; computes the effective 4D Higgs potential including tree-level contributions from gauge symmetry and one-loop radiative corrections inducing soft Z₂ breaking.
AI.result: Derives an automatically CP-conserving and Z₂-symmetric tree-level Higgs potential; generates soft Z₂-breaking mass terms at one-loop; shows that tuning brane-localized kinetic terms reproduces the observed 125 GeV SM Higgs mass while realizing electroweak symmetry breaking; predicts masses for the remaining physical Higgs scalars (e.g., heavy CP-even, CP-odd, and charged Higgses).
AI.conclusion: The orbifold GUT-inspired construction provides a natural, symmetry-driven origin for a viable two-Higgs-doublet structure, with predictive power for extended Higgs spectra and no fine-tuned discrete symmetries—offering a compelling alternative to bottom-up 2HDMs.

title: Next-to-next-to-leading order event generation for $t\bar{t}H$ production with approximate two-loop amplitude
comment: 36 pages, 10 figures, 3 tables, code available at https://powhegbox.mib.infn.it, manual provided as ancillary file
summary:
We study Higgs-boson production in association with a top-quark pair ($t\bar{t}H$) at hadron colliders and present the first matching of next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) QCD corrections to parton showers using the MiNNLOPS method. For the two-loop amplitude, we employ two established approximations, based on the soft Higgs-boson and high-energy limits, respectively. For the first time, we also construct the latter in full colour and propose a pointwise combination of the two approximations across phase space. By assigning a conservative uncertainty estimate, which remains well below the perturbative uncertainties, we ensure robust and reliable differential predictions, explicitly validated at the one-loop level. Apart from the two-loop amplitude, all remaining ingredients of the MiNNLOPS calculation are included exactly. After thorough validation, we present a series of phenomenological results illustrating the impact of NNLO corrections and parton-shower effects. We consider fiducial predictions for the Higgs-boson decay into photons and include off-shell top-quark decays with tree-level spin correlations in both the dilepton and semileptonic channels.
  Our $t\bar{t}H$ MiNNLOPS generator is publicly available within the POWHEG framework.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first NNLO QCD + parton shower matched prediction for tt̄H production using the MiNNLOPS method, combining two approximations of the two-loop amplitude (soft Higgs and high-energy limits) in full colour, with conservative uncertainty estimation and validation; includes off-shell top decays with spin correlations and is publicly available in POWHEG.
AI.motivation: To achieve robust, differential predictions for tt̄H production at hadron colliders by bridging the gap between fixed-order NNLO accuracy and realistic parton-shower modeling, while controlling theoretical uncertainties—particularly critical for precision Higgs coupling measurements and BSM searches.
AI.method: MiNNLOPS matching of NNLO QCD corrections to parton showers; uses two complementary approximations of the two-loop tt̄H amplitude—soft-Higgs and full-colour high-energy limit—combined pointwise across phase space; all other MiNNLOPS ingredients (e.g., real-emission, subtraction, Sudakov resummation) are exact; includes off-shell top decays with tree-level spin correlations in dilepton and semileptonic channels; validated at one-loop level and against known limits.
AI.result: First publicly available NNLO+PS matched generator for tt̄H within POWHEG; achieves subdominant uncertainty from amplitude approximations (well below perturbative scale uncertainties); yields improved differential predictions—especially for H→γγ in fiducial regions—and quantifies impact of NNLO corrections and parton-shower effects on key observables.
AI.conclusion: The MiNNLOPS tt̄H generator provides state-of-the-art, reliable, and publicly accessible predictions for LHC phenomenology; the hybrid amplitude approximation strategy is validated and uncertainty-controlled, setting a template for other multi-scale 2→3 processes.

title: Chiral-Maxwell Cavity EFT: Photon Condensation and Quantum-Optics Limits
summary: We develop an analytic, fully field-theoretic description of how a hadronic medium can induce photon condensation in a cavity. Starting from leading-order Chiral Perturbation Theory minimally coupled to Maxwell theory, we construct a consistent truncation to the lowest hadronic and gauge modes that still supports a non-vanishing baryon (topological) density. The resulting reduced dynamics is an effective \(1+1\) theory whose couplings retain memory of the three-dimensional cavity through discrete winding and transverse holonomy data. Integrating out the heavy hadronic mode at one loop yields a gauge-invariant effective potential for the lowest photonic cavity mode, from which we derive analytic criteria for a condensed window. In the opposite hierarchy, integrating out the gauge mode produces a one-loop deformation of a sine--Gordon-type EFT for the chiral mode and makes explicit where scale separation fails and the full coupled system must be kept. Upon quantization, the reduced theory maps onto standard nonlinear quantum-optics Hamiltonians, including a two-photon Rabi limit and quartic single-mode photonic models whose trivial and condensed branches obey distinct selection rules. This provides a concrete bridge between finite-density hadronic physics and experimentally familiar nonlinear-cavity diagnostics.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a field-theoretic framework linking finite-density hadronic physics (via chiral perturbation theory) to photon condensation in optical cavities, deriving analytic criteria for condensation and mapping the reduced dynamics to known nonlinear quantum-optics Hamiltonians.
AI.motivation: To establish a rigorous, analytic bridge between strongly interacting hadronic matter at finite baryon density and experimentally observable photon condensation phenomena in engineered cavities—bridging nuclear EFT and quantum optics.
AI.method: Starts from leading-order Chiral Perturbation Theory minimally coupled to Maxwell theory; constructs a consistent low-mode truncation retaining topological (baryon) density; reduces to an effective 1+1D theory with cavity-dependent holonomy/winding data; performs one-loop integration of heavy hadronic or photonic modes to obtain gauge-invariant effective potentials and deformed sine-Gordon EFT; quantizes to recover nonlinear quantum-optical Hamiltonians.
AI.result: Derives analytic criteria for a photon condensed window; reveals failure of scale separation when gauge mode is integrated out, necessitating full coupled treatment; maps quantized reduced theory to two-photon Rabi and quartic single-mode photonic Hamiltonians with distinct selection rules for trivial vs. condensed branches.
AI.conclusion: A fully field-theoretic, gauge-invariant description of cavity-induced photon condensation from hadronic matter is achieved, unifying chiral EFT, topological field theory, and quantum cavity QED—enabling first-principles interpretation of nonlinear-cavity experiments via hadronic parameters.

title: Black Hole Vision: An Interactive iOS Application for Visualizing Black Holes
summary: The Black Hole Explorer (BHEX) is a proposed mission to launch a sub-millimeter radio telescope into Earth orbit that will take the sharpest images in the history of astronomy and reveal novel horizon-scale features of supermassive black holes. Black Hole Vision is an open-source application, freely available on the iOS App Store, that produces lensed images which highlight the key features expected to appear in the black hole images BHEX will capture. The app combines video feeds from the front- and rear-facing iPhone cameras and uses the black hole lensing equations to synthesize an onscreen image displaying the user's surroundings as if they were gravitationally lensed by a black hole within the cameras' field of view. Here, we describe how light rays are lensed by non-rotating (Schwarzschild) and rotating (Kerr) black holes, and we list the equations needed for computing black-hole-lensed images. We also describe their specific implementation within Black Hole Vision.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Black Hole Vision, an iOS app that simulates gravitational lensing by Schwarzschild and Kerr black holes using real-time iPhone camera feeds, serving as an educational and visualization tool aligned with the upcoming BHEX space mission.
AI.motivation: To provide an accessible, interactive, and physically accurate visualization of black hole lensing for public engagement and education, bridging theoretical general relativity with anticipated observational results from the Black Hole Explorer (BHEX) mission.
AI.method: Derives and implements null geodesic equations for light propagation in Schwarzschild and Kerr spacetimes; integrates real-time front- and rear-camera video streams on iOS; applies ray-tracing via backward light tracing from screen pixels through the black hole metric to reconstruct lensed scenes.
AI.result: A functional, publicly available iOS application that renders real-time, metric-accurate gravitational lensing effects—including photon spheres, relativistic rings, and frame-dragging signatures—for both non-rotating and rotating black holes.
AI.conclusion: Black Hole Vision successfully translates complex relativistic optics into an intuitive, real-time augmented reality experience, demonstrating pedagogical value and serving as a precursor visualization framework for interpreting future high-resolution black hole imagery from BHEX.

title: The effects of Higgs boson couplings through HZZ production at future lepton colliders
summary: We focus on the sensitivity of the Higgs-gauge boson couplings at $HγZ$ and $HZZ$ vertices through the process $\ell^- \ell^+ \rightarrow HZZ$ at CLIC and Muon Collider. Signal and relevant backgrounds events are generated in MadGraph within Standard Model Effective Field Theory (SMEFT) framework. These events are passed through Pythia for parton showering, and realistic detector effects are simulated by Delphes. The limits at 95% C.L. on the coefficients $\overline{c}_{HB}$ and $\overline{c}_{HW}$ are obtained at three b-tagging working points; loose, medium, and tight containing the Delphes card from CLIC and Muon Collider, corresponding to b-tagging efficiencies of 90%, 70%, and 50%, respectively. We report that our best 95% C.L. limits on $\overline{c}_{HB}$ and $\overline{c}_{HW}$ coefficients are $[-0.00098; 0.00047]$ and $[-0.00158; 0.00008]$, respectively, at 3 TeV CLIC with an integrated luminosity of 5 ab$^{-1}$, and $[-0.00021; 0.00019]$ and $[-0.00019; 0.00007]$, respectively, at 10 TeV Muon Collider with an integrated luminosity of 10 ab$^{-1}$. These limits are compared with the present experimental and various phenomenological limits.

AI.tldr: This paper studies Higgs-gauge boson coupling sensitivity at HγZ and HZZ vertices via ℓ⁻ℓ⁺ → HZZ at CLIC and Muon Collider, using SMEFT, MadGraph, Pythia, and Delphes; it sets improved 95% C.L. limits on Wilson coefficients c̄_HB and c̄_HW.
AI.motivation: To probe deviations from the Standard Model in Higgs-gauge couplings—specifically HγZ and HZZ—via high-energy lepton colliders, leveraging SMEFT as a model-independent framework for BSM physics.
AI.method: Simulated ℓ⁻ℓ⁺ → HZZ signal and backgrounds in MadGraph within SMEFT; applied parton showering (Pythia) and detector simulation (Delphes) with CLIC and Muon Collider cards; extracted 95% C.L. limits on c̄_HB and c̄_HW using three b-tagging working points (loose/medium/tight) and profile likelihood analysis.
AI.result: At 3 TeV CLIC (5 ab⁻¹): c̄_HB ∈ [−0.00098, 0.00047], c̄_HW ∈ [−0.00158, 0.00008]; at 10 TeV Muon Collider (10 ab⁻¹): c̄_HB ∈ [−0.00021, 0.00019], c̄_HW ∈ [−0.00019, 0.00007]. These represent the strongest projected limits to date for these coefficients at lepton colliders.
AI.conclusion: The HZZ channel at high-energy lepton colliders provides exceptional sensitivity to dimension-6 SMEFT operators affecting HγZ and HZZ vertices; the Muon Collider yields tighter constraints than CLIC due to higher energy and luminosity, demonstrating its potential for precision Higgs EFT studies.

title: Higher-dimensional BKL dynamics in AdS black holes
summary: Chaotic BKL dynamics provides a canonical description of the approach to spacelike singularities as a sequence of Kasner epochs grouped into eras. While this paradigm is well established for cosmological singularities, explicit realizations inside black holes have been scarce, despite renewed interest from holography. Here, we construct a broad class of asymptotically AdS black holes in $D\ge 4$ whose interiors exhibit bona fide BKL dynamics as the singularity is approached. In the near-singularity regime, the evolution reduces to billiard-like motion in a compact domain that forms a regular $(D-2)$-simplex. We derive closed-form bouncing rules for the Kasner exponents in arbitrary dimension and prove the ensuing chaotic dynamics. A key novelty for $D\ge 5$ is a richer internal organization of eras: inequivalent transitions between epochs lead to distinct Kasner seasons, yielding new patterns of epoch/era structure for both electric and gravitational walls. Finally, we investigate a holographic diagnostic, the thermal $a$-function, whose monotonic flow captures individual epochs and eras and can display near-walking behavior in suitable Kasner regimes.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs asymptotically AdS black holes in D ≥ 4 whose interiors exhibit genuine BKL chaotic dynamics near the spacelike singularity, characterized by Kasner epochs and eras; it derives exact bouncing rules for Kasner exponents, proves chaos, reveals novel 'Kasner seasons' for D ≥ 5, and links epoch/era structure to holographic thermal a-function behavior.
AI.motivation: To provide explicit, physically realizable black hole interiors exhibiting bona fide Belinski–Khalatnikov–Lifshitz (BKL) chaotic dynamics—previously well-established for cosmological singularities but scarce in black hole contexts—especially motivated by holographic interest in singularity physics.
AI.method: Constructs asymptotically Anti-de Sitter (AdS) black hole solutions in D ≥ 4 dimensions; performs near-singularity asymptotic analysis to reduce Einstein equations to billiard motion in a compact domain (a regular (D−2)-simplex); derives closed-form Kasner exponent transition (bouncing) rules across gravitational and electric walls; analyzes era/epoch statistics and introduces 'Kasner seasons' for D ≥ 5; computes and interprets the thermal a-function as a holographic probe of local Kasner behavior.
AI.result: Demonstrates that a broad class of AdS black holes exhibits true BKL chaos near the singularity; obtains dimension-independent analytic Kasner transition rules; proves ergodicity/chaos of the billiard map; discovers new hierarchical era structure ('seasons') for D ≥ 5 due to inequivalent wall transitions; shows thermal a-function monotonically decreases per epoch/era and exhibits near-constant ('walking') behavior in specific Kasner regimes.
AI.conclusion: BKL dynamics is not merely cosmological but robustly realized in black hole interiors of asymptotically AdS gravity in D ≥ 4; the richer geometric and dynamical structure in D ≥ 5—especially Kasner seasons—implies deeper complexity in singularity resolution and holographic interpretation; the thermal a-function serves as a faithful, observable holographic signature of local Kasner evolution.

title: Multi-Segment Consistency Tests of General Relativity
summary:
As the LIGO-VIRGO-KAGRA Network of gravitational-wave detectors improves in sensitivity, accumulating hundreds of gravitational-wave detections per year, it becomes imperative to improve tests of general relativity in concert. The test of Hawking's law of area increase has gained prominence since GW250114, where black holes in General Relativity were tested with unprecedented precision, using the linear ringdown and pre-merger portions of the signal. A closely related test is the Inspiral-Merger-Ringdown Consistency Test, which assesses the consistency of the high- and low-frequency parts of the signals. In this letter, I present a multi-parameter Multi-Segment Consistency Test (MSCT) that generalizes and improves upon existing tests by ensuring that the extrinsic properties of the signal are consistent across its independent segments and by adopting an accelerated time-domain approach. The improved area law test is then presented as a projection of this MSCT test. These crucial improvements, which bring physical consistency to the area law test, lead to more stringent constraints on the increase in estimated area from observed binary black hole mergers, while also capturing covariances among the parameters.
  Applying the two-segment version of this test to the inspiral and ringdown parts of GW250114, and keeping some of the extrinsic parameters common between the segments, I test the signal to unprecedented accuracy, obtaining $4.61 ^{+0.24} _{-0.11}σ$ significant result for the area increase, even as more than 4 pre-merger cycles of the signal are excluded from the analysis. Also, I infer that the final state lies within the 15\% highest posterior density confidence interval.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Multi-Segment Consistency Test (MSCT) to rigorously test Hawking's area law and GR consistency in BBH mergers, applied to GW250114 with unprecedented 4.61σ significance for area increase while excluding early inspiral cycles.
AI.motivation: To improve physical consistency and statistical rigor in tests of general relativity—particularly Hawking's area law and Inspiral-Merger-Ringdown consistency—as gravitational-wave detector sensitivity increases and event rates grow.
AI.method: Develops a multi-parameter, time-domain Multi-Segment Consistency Test (MSCT) that enforces consistency of extrinsic parameters across independent signal segments (e.g., inspiral, ringdown); the area law test is formulated as a projection of MSCT; applies two-segment MSCT to GW250114 with shared extrinsic parameters and accelerated time-domain inference.
AI.result: Achieves 4.61^{+0.24}_{−0.11}σ significance for area increase in GW250114—even excluding >4 pre-merger cycles—and places the final black hole state within the top 15% highest posterior density interval; captures parameter covariances and yields tighter constraints than prior area-law tests.
AI.conclusion: MSCT provides a physically consistent, generalizable framework for testing GR with gravitational-wave signals, significantly strengthening the area law test and enabling more robust, multi-parameter consistency checks across signal segments.

title: An ode to instantons
comment: In memory of Sidney Coleman on the occasion of his 89th birthday
summary: We present a formalism for semiclassical time evolution in quantum mechanics, building on a century of work. We identify complex saddle points in real time, real saddle points in complex time, and complex saddle points in complex time that reproduce the known answers in classic problems. For the decay of a metastable state, we find finite time and finite energy analogs of the "bounce" which do not have strict zero or negative modes. The one-loop phase of the wave function and the multiplicity of bounce solutions at late times are discussed. The motivation of this work is to learn how to compute decay rates in quantum field theory in situations with non-trivial time dependence, by first taking a humble step backwards to the fascinating world of quantum mechanics.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a semiclassical formalism for time evolution in quantum mechanics using complex and real saddle points in time and/or configuration space, extending classic methods to finite-time/finite-energy decay processes (e.g., metastable state decay) without requiring zero/negative modes; it aims to lay groundwork for computing non-stationary decay rates in quantum field theory.
AI.motivation: To establish a robust semiclassical framework for computing decay rates in quantum field theory under non-trivial time dependence, starting from quantum mechanical analogs.
AI.method: Systematic identification and analysis of saddle points—complex in real time, real in complex time, and complex in complex time—in the semiclassical path integral; application to metastable state decay, including one-loop phase calculation and multiplicity analysis of bounce solutions.
AI.result: Discovery of finite-time and finite-energy analogs of the 'bounce' for metastable decay that lack strict zero or negative modes; characterization of the one-loop wave function phase and late-time multiplicity of bounce solutions.
AI.conclusion: The generalized saddle-point classification successfully reproduces known results in classic quantum mechanical problems and provides a flexible, mode-agnostic foundation for time-dependent semiclassical analysis, bridging quantum mechanics and future QFT applications.

title: Accelerated Time-domain Analysis for Gravitational Wave Astronomy
summary: Most current compact-binary searches and parameter-estimation pipelines evaluate the Gaussian-noise likelihood approximately using frequency-domain inner products with great success in analyzing gravitational-wave signals. This is historically motivated by (i) the approximate stationarity of detector noise on sufficiently long timescales, allowing a circulant approximation in the domain that diagonalizes the noise covariance in the Fourier basis, and (ii) the efficiency of matched filtering via fast Fourier transforms. However, the advantage of frequency-domain analysis comes with its own limitations. In this article, we develop a self-contained, end-to-end, \emph{fully time-domain} formulation of gravitational-wave inference and present an implementation that makes the likelihood evaluation practical at scale by exploiting structured linear algebra, software, and hardware acceleration. We validate the method using injections and demonstrate speedups for likelihood evaluation and on modern GPUs. We present \emph{tdanalysis}, an accelerated implementation that handles gaps, sharp boundaries, and multiple disjoint segments, and supports GPUs. We demonstrate some of its applications in gravitational wave astronomy.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a fully time-domain approach for gravitational-wave inference, overcoming limitations of traditional frequency-domain methods, and implements it in 'tdanalysis'—a GPU-accelerated, robust tool supporting gapped and segmented data.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of frequency-domain likelihood evaluation (e.g., assumptions of stationarity, difficulty handling data gaps and non-smooth boundaries) while retaining computational efficiency and scalability.
AI.method: Develops a self-contained, end-to-end time-domain formulation of the Gaussian-noise likelihood using structured linear algebra (e.g., hierarchical or Toeplitz-like solvers), combined with software optimizations and hardware acceleration (GPU support); handles non-stationary noise, data gaps, sharp boundaries, and multiple disjoint segments.
AI.result: Validated via signal injections; achieves significant speedups in likelihood evaluation on modern GPUs; demonstrates practical scalability and robustness in realistic GW analysis scenarios.
AI.conclusion: A fully time-domain inference framework is not only feasible but competitive—enabling more accurate modeling of non-stationary noise and complex data structures without sacrificing computational performance.

title: Representations of the Flat Space Wavefunction
summary: From any graph $G$ arises a flat space wavefunction, obtained by integrating a product of propagators associated to the vertices and edges of $G$. This function is a key ingredient in the computation of cosmological correlators, and several representations for it have been proposed. We formulate three such representations and prove their correctness. In particular, we show that the flat space wavefunction can be read off from the canonical form of the cosmological polytope, and we settle a conjecture of Fevola, Pimentel, Sattelberger, and Westerdijk regarding a partial fraction decomposition for the flat space wavefunction. The terms of the decomposition correspond to certain collections of connected subgraphs associated to $G$ and its spanning subgraphs, reflecting the fact that the flat space wavefunction contains information about how $G$ is connected.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes rigorous mathematical representations of the flat space wavefunction associated with a graph G, proving its equivalence to the canonical form of the cosmological polytope and confirming a conjecture on its partial fraction decomposition in terms of connected subgraphs.
AI.motivation: To provide mathematically rigorous foundations for flat space wavefunctions—key objects in computing cosmological correlators—and resolve an open conjecture about their combinatorial structure.
AI.method: The authors formulate and prove correctness of three representations of the flat space wavefunction: (i) via integration of propagators over graph vertices/edges, (ii) as the canonical form of the cosmological polytope associated with G, and (iii) as a partial fraction decomposition indexed by collections of connected subgraphs of G and its spanning subgraphs.
AI.result: They prove that the flat space wavefunction equals the canonical form of the cosmological polytope and confirm the Fevola–Pimentel–Sattelberger–Westerdijk conjecture, showing each term in the partial fraction decomposition corresponds bijectively to specific connected subgraph configurations encoding G's connectivity.
AI.conclusion: The flat space wavefunction is fully characterized both geometrically (via cosmological polytope canonical forms) and combinatorially (via connected subgraph decompositions), unifying physical interpretation with algebraic and polyhedral geometry.

title: Cyclic cosmology from Cuscuton-Gallileons subjected to Lie point transformations
summary: Spacetime transformations in any physically viable theory should follow Lie Point symmetry. In this work, we explore the Cuscuton model extended to Galileons, as introduced by de Rham et al in \cite{Rham2017}. We find the true degrees of freedom by converting the model into an equivalent first order model. Despite being a higher derivative model, it possesses only \textit{two} degrees of freedom. We calculate the Noether symmetry parameters corresponding to Lie point transformations, which lead to the vanishing of the original Cuscuton term's coefficient and restrict the potential to an exponential form. Interestingly, the coefficient corresponding to the original Cuscuton term vanish. Additionally, we also use the Killing analysis to find out the charges corresponding to the Killing vectors and the Killing tensors. The cosmological implications are examined through dynamical analysis, revealing that under the condition where the coefficient $a_2$ vanishes, the equation of state parameters exhibit damped oscillatory behavior .

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the extended Cuscuton–Galileon model using Lie point symmetries and Noether’s theorem, showing it has only two physical degrees of freedom despite higher derivatives; symmetry constraints force the original Cuscuton coefficient to vanish and fix the potential to exponential form; Killing analysis yields conserved charges; cosmological dynamics show damped oscillatory equation-of-state behavior when a2 = 0.
AI.motivation: To identify physically viable spacetime symmetries (Lie point) in higher-derivative scalar-tensor theories, specifically the de Rham et al. extended Cuscuton–Galileon model, and to determine its true dynamical content and cosmological behavior under symmetry constraints.
AI.method: Conversion to an equivalent first-order formalism to count degrees of freedom; computation of Noether symmetry parameters for Lie point transformations; imposition of symmetry conditions to constrain model parameters; Killing vector/tensor analysis to derive conserved charges; phase-space dynamical system analysis for cosmological evolution.
AI.result: The model contains exactly two propagating degrees of freedom; Noether symmetry requires vanishing of the original Cuscuton coupling coefficient and fixes the scalar potential to exponential form; conserved charges are obtained from Killing vectors and rank-2 Killing tensors; cosmological dynamical analysis shows damped oscillatory w(a) when a2 = 0.
AI.conclusion: Lie point symmetry strongly restricts the extended Cuscuton–Galileon model, selecting a minimal, physically consistent subclass with two degrees of freedom and exponential potential; the vanishing of the Cuscuton term indicates its non-essential role under symmetry requirements; the damped oscillatory equation of state suggests potentially viable late-time cosmic acceleration behavior.

title: Generalized transverse momentum distributions at small-$x$
summary: We compute the complete set of the leading-twist gluon and sea-quark generalized transverse momentum distributions (GTMDs) in the small-$x$, or eikonal, approximation at vanishing skewness $ξ= 0$. All the gluon GTMDs become expressed in terms of the basic gluon dipole operator featuring also proton helicity-flips. Consequently, we establish universal relations between otherwise distinct GTMDs that hold in the small-$x$ limit. The obtained results are systematically projected onto the transverse momentum dependent distributions (TMD) and the generalized parton distribution (GPD) cases, recovering known results where available. In case of sea-quarks all the GTMDs are given in terms of the gluon dipole convoluted with a hard kernel. We generalize the unpolarized sea-quark GTMD to non-zero momentum transfers and find new results for the proton helicity-flip distributions. We pay special attention to their perturbative, high transverse momentum, tails to confirm that it becomes governed by the small-$x$ gluon GPDs. The obtained results provide guidance to the phenomenological modeling of the GTMDs but also allow for their explicit computations.

AI.tldr: This paper computes leading-twist gluon and sea-quark GTMDs in the small-$x$ (eikonal) limit at zero skewness, expressing all gluon GTMDs via a universal gluon dipole operator—including helicity-flip contributions—and deriving universal relations among them; sea-quark GTMDs are expressed as gluon-dipole convolutions with hard kernels, with extensions to non-zero momentum transfer and new results for helicity-flip distributions; high-$k_T$ behavior is shown to be governed by small-$x$ gluon GPDs.
AI.motivation: To systematically determine the complete set of leading-twist GTMDs for gluons and sea quarks in the small-$x$ regime—where high-energy QCD dynamics dominate—and to uncover universal relations and connections to TMDs and GPDs for phenomenological modeling and first-principles computation.
AI.method: Uses the eikonal (small-$x$) approximation at vanishing skewness ($\xi = 0$); expresses GTMDs via light-front Wilson-line correlators; employs operator reduction to the fundamental gluon dipole operator (including helicity-flip matrix elements); performs systematic projections onto TMD and GPD limits; derives sea-quark GTMDs via gluon-dipole convolution with perturbative hard kernels; extends to non-zero transverse momentum transfer and analyzes high-$k_T$ asymptotics using collinear factorization.
AI.result: All leading-twist gluon GTMDs reduce to combinations of a single gluon dipole operator with proton helicity-flip dependence; universal small-$x$ relations among GTMDs are established; sea-quark GTMDs are fully expressed as gluon-dipole convolutions; new analytic results obtained for unpolarized and helicity-flip sea-quark GTMDs at non-zero $\Delta_\perp$; high-$k_T$ tails are proven to be controlled by small-$x$ gluon GPDs; known TMD/GPD limits are recovered consistently.
AI.conclusion: The small-$x$ limit provides a powerful unifying framework for GTMDs: gluon GTMDs are universally encoded in the gluon dipole, and sea-quark GTMDs inherit their structure from gluon dynamics via perturbative evolution; these results enable both constrained phenomenological parameterizations and ab initio lattice or model calculations of GTMDs in the high-energy regime.

title: Energy Extraction and Particle Acceleration in String-Inspired Rotating Einstein-Maxwell-Dilaton-Axion Black Hole
summary: We study energy extraction and particle acceleration in the rotating Einstein-Maxwell-Dilaton-Axion (EMDA) black hole, focusing on the impact of dilaton hair $b\le 0$ on near-horizon energetics relative to Kerr. For the Penrose process we derive analytic expressions for the maximum efficiency and show that negative $b$ can strongly enhance the ideal gain in the extremal regime (e.g., reaching $\sim 91\%$ for $b=-0.3$). We then compute the irreducible mass $M_{\rm irr}$ and the corresponding rotationally extractable energy $\mathcal{E}_{\rm rot}\equiv M-M_{\rm irr}$, finding that $M_{\rm irr}$ decreases monotonically as $b$ becomes more negative while $\mathcal{E}_{\rm rot}$ increases, indicating a larger spin-energy reservoir; at extremality the extracted share from rotation is $\mathcal{E}_{\rm rot}/M\simeq 0.63$ for EMDA, reducing to the Kerr value $\simeq 0.29$ at $b=0$. Kinematic constraints relevant to fragment production are quantified via the Wald and Bardeen--Press--Teukolsky bounds, which are progressively relaxed for more negative $b$. For wave superradiance we obtain the flux balance and the amplification window $0<β<kΩ_H$, with $Ω_H$ expressed through $Ξ=r_H^{2}+2br_H+a^{2}$; negative $b$ modifies $Ω_H$ and enlarges the parameter region exhibiting negative horizon flux. Finally, we analyse two-particle collisions and derive $E_{\rm cm}$, showing that the Bañados--Silk--West divergence persists at the horizon when one particle is tuned to the critical angular momentum $L_c=E/Ω_H$, while $E_{\rm cm}$ remains finite for generic angular momenta. Overall, dilaton hair in EMDA simultaneously amplifies energy-extraction channels and reshapes the near-horizon thresholds governing high-energy collisions.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how dilaton hair (parameter b ≤ 0) in rotating Einstein-Maxwell-Dilaton-Axion (EMDA) black holes enhances energy extraction and particle acceleration relative to Kerr black holes, analytically quantifying improvements in Penrose efficiency, rotational energy reservoir, superradiance window, and center-of-mass collision energy near the horizon.
AI.motivation: To understand how scalar hair (dilaton parameter b) modifies fundamental near-horizon energetics—energy extraction, irreducible mass, superradiance, and high-energy collisions—in rotating black holes beyond general relativity, particularly relative to the Kerr limit (b = 0).
AI.method: Analytical relativistic calculations: derivation of maximum Penrose efficiency; computation of irreducible mass M_irr and rotationally extractable energy ℰ_rot; application of Wald and Bardeen–Press–Teukolsky kinematic bounds for fragmentation; flux-balance analysis for wave superradiance; and geodesic two-particle collision analysis yielding center-of-mass energy E_cm at the horizon.
AI.result: Negative dilaton hair (b < 0) increases maximum Penrose efficiency up to ~91% (vs. ~20.7% in extremal Kerr), reduces M_irr, raises ℰ_rot/M to ~0.63 (vs. ~0.29 in Kerr), relaxes kinematic bounds for fragmentation, enlarges the superradiant amplification window via modified horizon angular velocity Ω_H, and preserves Bañados–Silk–West (BSW) divergence in E_cm for critical particles while keeping it finite generically.
AI.conclusion: Dilaton hair in EMDA black holes systematically enhances all major near-horizon energy-extraction mechanisms and lowers thresholds for high-energy particle interactions, demonstrating that scalar hair can significantly amplify black hole 'activity' beyond the Kerr paradigm.

title: Constraints on BMS Transformations via Energy Conditions and implications on black hole geometry
summary: We investigate whether the formally infinite-dimensional supertranslation sector of the Bondi-Metzner-Sachs (BMS) group remains fully physically admissible once classical energy conditions are enforced. Working in a perturbative framework $g_{ab}\rightarrow g_{ab}+h_{ab}$, we first develop a general toolkit by expanding the curvature tensors and the Ricci scalar in powers of the perturbation $h_{ab}$ and recast the strong, weak, null and dominant energy conditions (SEC, WEC, NEC and DEC, respectively) as explicit inequalities on $h_{ab}$ following from the Raychaudhuri equation. The formalism is general, but to obtain concrete constraints we specialize to the standard BMS form on a Schwarzschild background and parametrize $h_{ab}=\mathcal{L}_ηg_{ab} $ by a supertranslation function $f(θ,φ)$. We find that the SEC and WEC impose nontrivial angular restrictions on $f$ already at next-to-leading order (NLO) in the perturbation, whereas the NEC and DEC are preserved at linear order and acquire their first nontrivial contributions only at next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO). Notably, the NNLO NEC reduces to a purely angular condition (independent of the radial coordinate), providing the strongest constraint on admissible supertranslations. Thus, imposing energy conditions substantially reduces the space of physically admissible supertranslations; the allowed sector, although remains infinite-dimensional in principle, is substantially constrained in practice.

AI.tldr: This paper examines how classical energy conditions constrain the infinite-dimensional supertranslation symmetry of the BMS group in asymptotically flat spacetimes, using perturbative gravity on a Schwarzschild background; it finds that energy conditions—especially the null energy condition at NNLO—impose strong angular restrictions on supertranslation functions, significantly reducing the physically admissible sector.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the formally infinite-dimensional supertranslation sector of the BMS group remains physically viable when classical energy conditions (SEC, WEC, NEC, DEC) are imposed, addressing potential tensions between asymptotic symmetries and energy constraints in general relativity.
AI.method: Perturbative analysis of Einstein gravity: expands curvature tensors and Ricci scalar to second order in metric perturbation h_ab; expresses energy conditions via the Raychaudhuri equation as inequalities on h_ab; specializes to BMS-form perturbations on Schwarzschild background with h_ab = £_η g_ab generated by a supertranslation vector field parametrized by f(θ,φ); computes leading-order violations/constraints order-by-order (LO, NLO, NNLO).
AI.result: SEC and WEC impose nontrivial angular constraints on f(θ,φ) already at NLO; NEC and DEC remain satisfied at linear order but acquire first nontrivial constraints at NNLO; crucially, the NNLO NEC reduces to a purely angular inequality—strongest and most restrictive—effectively pruning the space of admissible supertranslations.
AI.conclusion: Classical energy conditions substantially restrict the physically allowed supertranslations: while the admissible sector remains infinite-dimensional in principle, it is strongly constrained in practice—particularly by the NNLO null energy condition—challenging the full physical realizability of the formal BMS supertranslation symmetry.

title: Threshold resummation for gluon fusion $ZH$ production at the LHC
summary: We present precise results for the $ZH$ production cross-section and invariant mass distribution at the LHC, taking into account the effects of the leading and sub-leading soft gluons. We improve both quark-initiated and gluon-initiated subprocesses through threshold resummation within the QCD framework.

AI.tldr: This paper computes high-precision QCD predictions for $ZH$ production at the LHC, incorporating threshold resummation of soft gluons for both quark- and gluon-initiated channels.
AI.motivation: To improve theoretical precision of $ZH$ production cross-sections and kinematic distributions at the LHC by accounting for dominant soft-gluon effects near partonic threshold.
AI.method: Threshold resummation in QCD to all orders in the strong coupling, applied separately to quark-initiated ($q\bar{q} \to ZH$) and gluon-initiated ($gg \to ZH$) subprocesses; matched to fixed-order calculations for the invariant mass distribution and total cross-section.
AI.result: Precise predictions for the $ZH$ production cross-section and $m_{ZH}$ invariant mass spectrum at the LHC, with reduced scale uncertainties and improved agreement with potential high-statistics data.
AI.conclusion: Soft-gluon resummation significantly enhances the accuracy of $ZH$ predictions, especially near threshold; inclusion of both quark- and gluon-initiated channels is essential for a complete next-to-next-to-leading-logarithmic (NNLL) description.

title: On the non-commutativity of geometric observables in different Lorentz frames
summary: Our aim is to establish whether geometric observables, such as length, area or volume of a physical object, viewed by different observers Poisson commute or not. To illustrate this, we compute the Poisson bracket of two lengths associated to a rigid rod and measured by two different geodesic (inertial) observers, one of which is at rest while the other is moving with respect to the rod. Our calculation shows that geometric observables measured by different observers generically do not Poisson commute, not even in Minkowski spacetime. This non-trivial result provides interesting insights into questions related to the presence of a fundamental scale in the context of quantum gravity.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether geometric observables (e.g., length, area, volume) measured by different inertial observers Poisson commute; it finds they generally do not—even in flat Minkowski spacetime—implying potential implications for quantum gravity and fundamental scales.
AI.motivation: To assess whether geometric observables measured by distinct observers Poisson commute, addressing foundational questions about observables and the role of a fundamental scale in quantum gravity.
AI.method: Computes the Poisson bracket of two length measurements of a rigid rod made by two geodesic (inertial) observers—one at rest, one moving—in Minkowski spacetime.
AI.result: Geometric observables measured by different observers generically fail to Poisson commute, even in Minkowski spacetime.
AI.conclusion: The non-commutativity of geometric observables across observers is generic and suggests that such observables cannot be simultaneously sharp in a canonical quantization framework, bearing relevance for quantum gravity phenomenology and the notion of a fundamental spacetime scale.

title: The Higgs boson in the CP-violating NB-LSSM
summary: This study investigates the lightest and second-lightest Higgs bosons at around 95 GeV (which is only a hypothetical scenario) and 125 GeV respectively within the CP-violating next to minimum B-L supersymmetric model(NB-LSSM). In the NB-LSSM, the CP violation in the Higgs potential leads to the mixing mass terms between the CP-even and CP-odd Higgs fields. Thus, one has to consider a $(10 \times 10)$-dimensional mass matrix for the neutral Higgs boson. These potential mixing effects may lead to drastic variations on the neutral Higgs boson masses. Besides, the neutral Higgs bosons predicted in the NB-LSSM may strongly mix with one another, thereby significantly modifying the couplings of the Higgs bosons to the fermions or gauge bosons. It is found that the specific parameters $g_{YB}$, $\tanβ$, $T_κ$, $T_λ, \cdot\cdot\cdot$ and CP-violating phases $θ_{1,2,3,4,6,7,8}$ in the NB-LSSM affect the theoretical predictions on the Higgs boson mass and corresponding signal strengthes significantly. And the theoretical predictions on the signal strengthes of SM-like Higgs decay channels and excess signals at around 95 GeV are fitted well to the observed experimental data.

AI.tldr: This paper studies Higgs boson mass and coupling predictions in the CP-violating NB-LSSM, focusing on ~95 GeV and 125 GeV states; it constructs a 10×10 neutral Higgs mass matrix due to CP-violating mixing, identifies key parameters and phases governing mass splitting and signal strengths, and shows consistency with LHC data for both the SM-like Higgs and the 95 GeV excess.
AI.motivation: To explain the observed ~95 GeV Higgs-like excess while maintaining consistency with the established 125 GeV Higgs, within a theoretically motivated B-L supersymmetric extension that incorporates CP violation in the Higgs sector.
AI.method: Constructs and diagonalizes a (10 × 10) neutral Higgs mass matrix arising from CP-violating terms in the NB-LSSM Higgs potential; performs parameter scans over gauge couplings (g_YB), tanβ, soft trilinear couplings (T_κ, T_λ), and eight CP-violating phases (θ₁,₂,₃,₄,₆,₇,₈); computes Higgs masses, mixings, and decay signal strengths, comparing them to LHC measurements.
AI.result: CP violation induces significant off-diagonal mixing between CP-even and CP-odd states, drastically altering neutral Higgs masses and couplings; specific combinations of g_YB, tanβ, T_κ, T_λ, and θ₁,₂,₃,₄,₆,₇,₈ reproduce both m_h ≈ 125 GeV with SM-compatible signal strengths and an additional light state at ~95 GeV with enhanced diphoton and bb̄γγ rates matching experimental excesses.
AI.conclusion: The CP-violating NB-LSSM provides a viable theoretical framework accommodating the 95 GeV excess alongside the 125 GeV Higgs without violating current constraints, demonstrating that extended SUSY Higgs sectors with CP violation offer testable alternatives to the Standard Model.

title: On Schwarzschild black hole singularity formation
summary: We examine whether the Schwarzschild black hole can emerge as the continuous end state of gravitational collapse from a non-singular configuration. Employing a time dependent extension of the regular Schwarzschild metric, we track the evolution of the geometry during collapse and find that the process cannot remain continuous. The metric function develops a discontinuity at the origin, marking a breakdown of spacetime smoothness, an effect identified as ``Minkowski breaking.'' Before the Schwarzschild point source can form at $r=0$, curvature singularities appear and the Cauchy horizon disappears. These results strongly suggest that spacetime may not evolve smoothly toward the Schwarzschild geometry. Instead, the formation of a Schwarzschild black hole appears to entail a discrete change in the structure of spacetime, pointing to the need for a noncontinuous, possibly quantized, framework to describe the emergence or regularization of gravitational singularities.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether Schwarzschild black holes can form smoothly from nonsingular gravitational collapse, and finds that spacetime continuity breaks down before the singularity forms—termed 'Minkowski breaking'—implying a discrete, possibly quantum, transition is required.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the Schwarzschild black hole can arise as a continuous, nonsingular end state of gravitational collapse, challenging the classical smoothness assumption near r=0.
AI.method: Uses a time-dependent extension of the regular Schwarzschild metric to model dynamical collapse and analyzes the evolution of metric functions and causal structure, focusing on continuity, curvature singularities, and Cauchy horizon behavior.
AI.result: The metric function develops a discontinuity at r=0 ('Minkowski breaking'); curvature singularities form before the point source is reached, and the Cauchy horizon vanishes—indicating loss of spacetime smoothness and breakdown of deterministic evolution.
AI.conclusion: Schwarzschild black hole formation cannot occur via a continuous classical process; instead, it requires a discrete structural change in spacetime, suggesting the necessity of a noncontinuous or quantized description of singularity emergence/regularization.

title: Threshold Resummation of Drell-Yan type colorless processes at LHC
summary: We look at the threshold effects in neutral and charged Drell-Yan production, Higgs boson production with a massive vector boson, and Higgs production in bottom quark annihilation at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC), up to the third order in QCD. Using third-order soft-virtual (SV) results and the universal properties of threshold logarithms, we find the process-dependent coefficients and improve the accuracy by including large threshold logarithms up to next-to-next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic (N$^3$LL) order and matched with the latest N$^3$LO results. We also show numerical results for the invariant mass distributions and total production cross sections for these processes. Our findings show that the theoretical scale uncertainties, which are about $0.4\%$ at N$^3$LO in fixed-order calculations, decrease to less than $0.1\%$ at N$^3$LO+N$^3$LL after SV threshold resummation in the high invariant mass region.

AI.tldr: This paper improves theoretical predictions for Drell-Yan and Higgs production processes at the LHC by combining third-order QCD calculations with threshold resummation up to N³LL accuracy, reducing scale uncertainties from ~0.4% to <0.1% in high invariant mass regions.
AI.motivation: To reduce theoretical scale uncertainties in high-precision QCD predictions for key LHC processes—neutral/charged Drell-Yan, Higgs+vector boson, and Higgs via b-quark annihilation—by systematically including large threshold logarithms beyond fixed-order perturbation theory.
AI.method: Combines third-order soft-virtual (SV) QCD results with universal threshold logarithm structure to determine process-dependent coefficients; performs N³LL resummation and matches to full N³LO fixed-order calculations; computes invariant mass distributions and total cross sections.
AI.result: Scale uncertainties drop from ~0.4% at N³LO to <0.1% at N³LO+N³LL in the high invariant mass region; precise predictions obtained for differential and integrated cross sections across all considered processes.
AI.conclusion: Threshold resummation at N³LL accuracy significantly enhances precision of QCD predictions for electroweak and Higgs processes at the LHC, enabling more stringent tests of the Standard Model and improved sensitivity to new physics.

title: Quantum Big Bounce in Wheeler-DeWitt scattering theory: Ekpyrotic and LQC-like transitions
summary: We present a rigorous formulation of the Quantum Big Bounce for the closed isotropic Universe, filled with a self-interacting scalar field, that emerges from the interaction with an ekpyrotic potential. Working in a covariant approach to the minisuperspace, we demonstrate the quantum equivalence between parametrizations in terms of the logarithmic scale factor and the volume variable. The analogy between the Wheeler-DeWitt equation and the Klein-Gordon equation, alongside a proper definition of asymptotic states, allows the identification of two different bouncing scenarios: one in which the transition occurs over a fixed direction of the internal time arrow, corresponding to a LQC-like scenario, and one involving a reversal of the internal time flow. The high-energy divergence in the former case shows the incompleteness of the WDW theory and the need for regularization. Therefore, the WDW theory is valid up to a given energy threshold. The latter transition, corresponding to an ekpyrotic scenario, is instead well-posed at any energy scale at the first perturbative order. While the Ashtekar school Big Bounce is expected to be recovered when high-energy corrections are included in this scheme, the WDW alone can avoid the cosmological singularity in a quantum mechanical fashion.

AI.tldr: This paper rigorously formulates the Quantum Big Bounce for a closed, isotropic universe with a self-interacting scalar field and ekpyrotic potential, showing quantum equivalence between logarithmic scale factor and volume parametrizations; it identifies two bouncing scenarios—one LQC-like with internal time preservation (but UV-incomplete), and one ekpyrotic with internal time reversal (well-posed at all energies to first order)—and concludes that the Wheeler–DeWitt theory alone can resolve the cosmological singularity quantum mechanically.
AI.motivation: To resolve the cosmological singularity via quantum gravity in a closed isotropic universe with ekpyrotic dynamics, and to clarify the domain of validity and physical interpretation of the Wheeler–DeWitt (WDW) formalism in bouncing cosmologies.
AI.method: Covariant minisuperspace quantization; identification of quantum equivalence between ln(a) and V parametrizations; analogy between the Wheeler–DeWitt equation and the Klein–Gordon equation; definition of asymptotic states to distinguish bouncing regimes; perturbative analysis of high-energy behavior.
AI.result: Two distinct quantum bounce scenarios are identified: (i) a LQC-like bounce preserving internal time direction but suffering high-energy divergence (indicating WDW incompleteness without regularization), and (ii) an ekpyrotic bounce with internal time reversal, well-posed at all energy scales to first perturbative order. The WDW theory alone suffices to avoid the singularity quantum mechanically.
AI.conclusion: The Wheeler–DeWitt framework—despite its UV limitations in certain parametrizations—can consistently describe a nonsingular quantum bounce when appropriately interpreted (e.g., via ekpyrotic time reversal); this suggests that singularity resolution does not strictly require loop quantum gravity corrections, though they may recover the Ashtekar-school Big Bounce in the full theory.

title: Revisiting unitarity of single scalar field with non-minimal coupling
summary: We have investigated the unitarity violation scale of a non-minimally coupled scalar field with quartic self-coupling. This model is widely studied in the literature but the estimation of the unitarity violation scale has not been consistently discussed, especially in the Jordan frame. We have calculated the six-point scattering amplitudes of the scalar particles in both the Jordan frame and the Einstein frame, and explicitly shown the frame-independence of the results. Since the extended target space with the conformal mode is trivial in the single-field case, the dominant contribution comes from the potential of the scalar field. The results in both frames become trivial in the vanishing self-coupling limit as expected.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the unitarity violation scale of a non-minimally coupled scalar field with quartic self-coupling, computing six-point scattering amplitudes in both Jordan and Einstein frames and demonstrating their frame independence.
AI.motivation: Inconsistent estimation of the unitarity violation scale for non-minimally coupled scalar fields—particularly in the Jordan frame—despite widespread study of the model.
AI.method: Calculation of six-point scalar scattering amplitudes in both Jordan and Einstein frames; explicit verification of frame independence; analysis of dominant contributions from the scalar potential, especially in the extended target space including the conformal mode.
AI.result: Frame-independent unitarity violation scale determined; amplitudes reduce to trivial (vanishing) form in the limit of zero quartic self-coupling; dominant contribution confirmed to arise from the scalar potential.
AI.conclusion: The unitarity violation scale is robustly defined and frame-independent; the Jordan-frame analysis is consistent with Einstein-frame results, resolving prior ambiguities in the literature.

title: Hybrid quantum-classical simulations of semiclassical gravity
summary: We propose a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm for the simulation of real-time dynamics in interacting quantum field theories coupled to classical fields, focusing on the self-consistent estimation of semiclassical backreaction. By discretizing space and time, we construct an iterative protocol that simulates the Trotterized dynamics of the quantum fields subject to the dynamical classical fields. By estimating certain quantum expectation values through a set of projective measurements, we source the equations of motion of the classical fields, and solve them numerically to feed them forward to the quantum simulation in an iterative self-consistent loop. Semiclassical backreaction is relevant in various fields of physics, particularly in cosmology, where quantum matter fluctuations affect the gravitational field dynamics, and a controlled renormalization must be carefully considered to get a sensible continuum limit. We benchmark our algorithm in this context, focusing on scalar-tensor theories of modified gravity exhibiting a chameleon mechanism, such that a light classical scalar field driving cosmic acceleration becomes massive in high-density regions, effectively screening any possible yet unobserved fifth force. By focusing on numerically tractable regimes, we explicitly show the convergence and robustness of our algorithm when considering the continuum limit and the effect of quantum shot noise. Our work paves the way for future experiments exploring other non-tractable regimes, including non-perturbative interactions of the quantum fields and how these can change backreaction and the gravitational dynamics.

AI.tldr: Proposes a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm to simulate real-time dynamics of interacting quantum field theories coupled to classical fields, with self-consistent semiclassical backreaction; benchmarks on chameleon scalar-tensor gravity, demonstrating convergence, robustness under shot noise, and proper renormalization in the continuum limit.
AI.motivation: To enable controlled simulation of semiclassical backreaction—where quantum matter fluctuations dynamically affect classical fields (e.g., gravity in cosmology)—which is analytically intractable in non-perturbative regimes and requires careful renormalization for a physical continuum limit.
AI.method: Discretizes spacetime, employs Trotterized quantum evolution for quantum fields conditioned on classical fields, estimates quantum expectation values via projective measurements to source classical equations of motion, and solves them numerically in a self-consistent feedback loop; applies to chameleon scalar-tensor theories with density-dependent screening.
AI.result: Demonstrates numerical convergence and robustness against quantum shot noise in tractable regimes; validates proper handling of renormalization and continuum limit; establishes feasibility for future exploration of non-perturbative quantum interactions affecting backreaction.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid algorithm provides a scalable, self-consistent framework for simulating semiclassical dynamics in quantum field theory–classical field couplings, with immediate applicability to cosmological backreaction problems and potential extension to strong-coupling and non-perturbative regimes.

title: QCD corrections for Pseudoscalar Higgs decay to 3 partons at higher orders in dimensional regulator
summary: In this contribution, we present our recent study on the second-order corrections of pseudo-scalar($A$) Higgs decay to three partons, at higher orders in the dimensional regulator. We have studied the one and two-loop amplitudes for processes, $A\to ggg$ and $A\to q\bar{q}g$ in the effective theory framework. Our results, after suitable crossings of the external momenta, are important ingredients for predicting the differential distribution of the pseudo-scalar Higgs boson in association with a jet at hadron colliders.

AI.tldr: This paper computes second-order QCD corrections to pseudo-scalar Higgs decays into three partons (ggg and q ar{q}g) at one- and two-loop levels in an effective theory, enabling improved predictions for A + jet production at hadron colliders.
AI.motivation: To improve theoretical precision for pseudo-scalar Higgs boson production in association with a jet at hadron colliders, requiring higher-order QCD corrections to its decay processes.
AI.method: Computed one- and two-loop amplitudes for A → ggg and A → q\bar{q}g in the dimensionally regularized effective field theory framework, using analytic techniques appropriate for multi-leg loop calculations.
