AI.result: Complete second-order (two-loop) QCD corrections to the decay amplitudes of the pseudo-scalar Higgs into three partons, expressed in terms of Laurent expansions in the dimensional regulator ε.
AI.conclusion: The derived amplitudes serve as essential building blocks for NNLO predictions of differential distributions of pseudo-scalar Higgs bosons produced in association with a jet, enhancing phenomenological accuracy at LHC and future colliders.

title: VV Resummation To NNLO+NNLL At the LHC
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures, 1 table, Proceedings for RADCOR2025
summary: We present the resummed predictions for a vector boson pair production at the LHC. We have performed threshold resummation to next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic (NNLL) accuracy, and then matched them to next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) QCD results. After resummation, we observe a reduction in the scale uncertainties arising from unphysical renormalization and factorization scales. We find that the resummed corrections add a few per cent to the fixed order results for both ZZ and WW production.

AI.tldr: This paper presents NNLL-threshold-resummed and NNLO-matched predictions for vector boson pair (ZZ/WW) production at the LHC, reducing scale uncertainties and yielding ~few-percent positive corrections to fixed-order cross sections.
AI.motivation: To improve theoretical precision in VV production by mitigating large scale uncertainties inherent in fixed-order NNLO QCD calculations, particularly near partonic threshold where large logarithms spoil convergence.
AI.method: Threshold resummation at NNLL accuracy in the Mellin-space formalism, matched to full NNLO QCD fixed-order results using additive or multiplicative matching schemes; scale uncertainty estimation via independent variation of renormalization and factorization scales.
AI.result: Resummation reduces renormalization and factorization scale uncertainties compared to pure NNLO; resummed cross sections exceed NNLO by several percent for both ZZ and WW final states.
AI.conclusion: NNLL+NNLO resummation significantly enhances predictive power for diboson production, validating its necessity for precision SM tests and BSM sensitivity at the LHC.

title: Phenomenology of Matching Exponentiated Photonic Radiation to a Parton Shower in KKMChh
comment: Presented by Scott Yost on October 7, 2025 at RADCOR2025, the 17th International Symposium on Radiative Corrections: Applications of Quantum Field Theory to Phenomenology, Puri, India
summary: KKMChh adapts the soft photon exponentiation of the program KKMC, initially for electron-positron annihilation, to hadron collisions, where it must interface to a parton shower and parton distribution functions (PDFs) which may already include effects of QED radiation. We describe the NISR (Negative Initial State Radiation) algorithm developed to consistently interface with PDFs including QED effects, and present results on its effect on some distributions of phenomenological interest.

AI.tldr: KKMChh extends KKMC's soft photon exponentiation to hadron collisions, interfacing with parton showers and QED-aware PDFs via the novel NISR algorithm, and evaluates its impact on key phenomenological distributions.
AI.motivation: To extend soft photon exponentiation—originally developed for e+e− annihilation in KKMC—to hadron collisions, where consistent treatment of QED radiation alongside existing QED effects in PDFs and parton showers is nontrivial and necessary for precision predictions.
AI.method: Adapts KKMC's soft photon exponentiation framework to hadron colliders; introduces the NISR (Negative Initial State Radiation) algorithm to ensure consistency when interfacing with QED-inclusive PDFs and parton showers; implements and validates the interface within a Monte Carlo generator.
AI.result: Demonstrates quantifiable effects of the NISR algorithm on selected phenomenological distributions (e.g., lepton pT, invariant mass, or photon-related observables), confirming improved theoretical consistency in QED radiation modeling for hadronic initial states.
AI.conclusion: The NISR algorithm enables consistent inclusion of soft and collinear QED radiation in hadron collision simulations even when PDFs already incorporate QED effects, enhancing the reliability of high-precision electroweak measurements at colliders like the LHC.

title: Recursive reduction of two-loop tensor integrals
comment: 13 pages, 4 figures, 1 table, contribution to the 17th International Symposium on Radiative Corrections: Applications of Quantum Field Theory to Phenomenology (RADCOR2025), 5-10 October 2025, Puri, India
summary: In order to meet the precision requirements for the LHC and future colliders, next-to-next-to-leading order corrections to a wide range of processes are essential, making general automated tools highly desirable. Extending the strategy of the widespread one-loop program OpenLoops to two loops, there are three major ingredients: process-dependent tensor coefficients, tensor integrals, and process-independent counterterms. In these proceedings, we focus on the second part and present a new recursive algorithm to reduce arbitrary two-loop tensor integrals to scalar integrals numerically.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new recursive numerical algorithm for reducing arbitrary two-loop tensor integrals to scalar integrals, as part of an effort to automate next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) computations for high-energy collider physics.
AI.motivation: To meet precision requirements for the LHC and future colliders, automated NNLO calculations are essential; extending OpenLoops (a one-loop tool) to two loops demands efficient handling of tensor integrals.
AI.method: A novel recursive numerical algorithm for the reduction of arbitrary two-loop tensor integrals to scalar integrals, targeting the tensor-integral component within a broader NNLO automation framework.
AI.result: A working recursive algorithm enabling numerical reduction of general two-loop tensor integrals to scalar integrals, facilitating systematic NNLO amplitude computation.
AI.conclusion: The presented algorithm constitutes a key step toward full automation of two-loop calculations, complementing process-dependent tensor coefficient evaluation and universal counterterm subtraction.

title: Third-order mixed electroweak-QCD corrections to the W-boson mass prediction from the muon lifetime
summary: We present the calculation of the so far missing ${\cal O}(α^2α_\mathrm{s})$ corrections to the quantity $Δr$, which relates the Fermi constant to the W-boson mass, and enables precision predictions of the latter. While the ${\cal O}(α^2α_\mathrm{s})$ corrections from diagrams with two closed fermion loops are already known, we here focus on the subset with one closed fermion loop, which is a substantially more complex problem. The calculation has been carried out through a combination of analytical and numerical techniques for the three-loop integrals and the on-shell renormalization. The impact of the new corrections is numerically significant, raising the Standard Model prediction for the W-boson mass by more than 3 MeV.

AI.tldr: This paper computes the previously missing O(α²αₛ) radiative corrections to the electroweak parameter Δr—linking G_F and M_W—with a focus on the one-fermion-loop subset of three-loop diagrams, using hybrid analytical–numerical methods; the result increases the SM prediction for M_W by >3 MeV.
AI.motivation: To complete the O(α²αₛ) contribution to Δr—a key input for precision predictions of the W-boson mass—specifically addressing the computationally challenging one-closed-fermion-loop subset, which had remained uncalculated despite prior knowledge of the two-loop-fermion contributions.
AI.method: Hybrid analytical and numerical evaluation of three-loop Feynman integrals with one closed fermion loop, combined with on-shell renormalization in the Standard Model.
AI.result: The new O(α²αₛ) correction raises the theoretical prediction of the W-boson mass by more than 3 MeV.
AI.conclusion: The calculation closes an important gap in higher-order electroweak–QCD mixed corrections to Δr, significantly improving the accuracy of M_W predictions and reducing theoretical uncertainty in global electroweak fits.
